topics.title: Data transfer/exchange between specified memory locations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL508 - MICROCONTROLLER LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Data transfer/exchange between specified memory locations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Data Transfer / Data Exchange** in the context of the 8051 Microcontroller refers to the movement (copy) or mutual swapping of 8-bit data between specified memory locations using Assembly Language Programming (ALP). The operations exploit the 8051's three physically distinct address spaces: **Internal RAM (00H–7FH)**, **Special Function Registers (80H–FFH)**, and **External Data Memory (0000H–FFFFH)**. The instructions used belong primarily to the `MOV`, `MOVX`, `MOVC`, `XCH`, `XCHD`, `PUSH`, and `POP` instruction families.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *Data transfer* involves copying the contents of a source memory location into a destination location without altering the source. *Data exchange* involves mutually swapping the contents of two locations atomically in a single operation (XCH) or nibble-wise (XCHD).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of an office with **three storage rooms**:

| Storage Area | Analogy | 8051 Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| The desk drawer | Small, fast, items you grab instantly | **Internal RAM** (00H–7FH, 128 bytes) |
| The locked cabinet | Restricted-access drawers | **SFRs** (80H–FFH) |
| The warehouse across town | Large but slow to reach | **External RAM** (up to 64 KB) |

- **Transfer (MOV)** = "Make a photocopy of file-A and put it into drawer-B." The original in drawer-A remains untouched.
- **Exchange (XCH)** = "Swap the contents of drawer-A and drawer-B in one quick motion." Both locations get a new value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Almost every embedded application (sensor logging, UART buffering, LCD driving, EEPROM emulation) begins with a block-move of data. Mastering this experiment builds the foundation for loops, pointer registers `R0/R1/DPTR`, and indirect addressing — all of which are tested directly in the KTU ESE.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value (KTU Lab Default) |
|---|---|
| Crystal Frequency ($f_{osc}$) | **11.0592 MHz** (chosen for exact 9600 baud UART generation) |
| Oscillator Period ($T_{osc} = 1/f_{osc}$) | **90.42 ns** |
| Machine Cycle ($T_{MC} = 12 \times T_{osc}$) | **1.085 µs** |
| Internal RAM Size | **128 Bytes** (00H–7FH) for standard AT89C51 |
| Register Banks | 4 banks × 8 bytes (00H–1FH) |
| Bit-addressable Region | 16 bytes (20H–2FH) → **128 addressable bits** |
| External Data Memory | Up to **64 KB** (0000H–FFFFH) via MOVX |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051 Memory Architecture (Three Address Spaces)

The 8051 uses a **Harvard-style** memory map. Data transfer instructions must respect which space they target:

1. **Internal Data Memory (IDATA / DATA):** Addressed directly (00H–7FH) or via indirect `@R0/@R1`. Used for variables, stack, and registers.
2. **Special Function Registers (SFRs):** Addressed only directly (80H–FFH). Includes `ACC (E0H)`, `B (F0H)`, `PSW (D0H)`, `SP (81H)`, `DPH (83H)`, `DPL (82H)`, `P0–P3`.
3. **External Data Memory (XDATA):** Addressed via `@DPTR` (16-bit) or `@R0/@R1` (8-bit, page-wise with P2). Accessed only through `MOVX`.
4. **Program Memory (CODE):** Accessed only via `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` or `MOVC A, @A+PC` for table look-ups.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Viva Hot-Question:** *"Why does the 8051 use different instructions for different memories?"* — Because the read/write control signals (RD, PSEN) differ, and the bus architecture is Harvard. The CPU must explicitly tell the hardware which space to activate.

## 2.2 Instruction Categories Relevant to Experiment 2

### A. The MOV Family (Transfer)
| Instruction | Operation | Addressing Modes | Machine Cycles | Bytes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `MOV A, Rn` | A ← Rn | Register | 1 | 1 |
| `MOV A, direct` | A ← (direct) | Direct | 1 | 2 |
| `MOV A, @Ri` | A ← (Ri) | Indirect | 1 | 1 |
| `MOV A, #data` | A ← #data | Immediate | 1 | 2 |
| `MOV direct, A` | (direct) ← A | Direct | 1 | 2 |
| `MOV direct, #data` | (direct) ← #data | Direct-Immediate | 2 | 3 |
| `MOV Rn, #data` | Rn ← #data | Immediate | 1 | 2 |
| `MOV @Ri, A` | (Ri) ← A | Indirect | 1 | 1 |
| `MOV DPTR, #data16` | DPTR ← 16-bit | Immediate | 2 | 3 |

### B. The MOVX Family (External RAM Transfer)
| Instruction | Operation | Addressing Mode |
|---|---|---|
| `MOVX A, @DPTR` | A ← external[DPTR] | Indirect-16 |
| `MOVX @DPTR, A` | external[DPTR] ← A | Indirect-16 |
| `MOVX A, @Ri` | A ← external[P2:Ri] | Indirect-8 (page) |
| `MOVX @Ri, A` | external[P2:Ri] ← A | Indirect-8 (page) |

### C. The MOVC Family (Code Memory Read)
| Instruction | Operation |
|---|---|
| `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | A ← code[A + DPTR] |
| `MOVC A, @A+PC` | A ← code[A + PC] |

### D. The Exchange Family
| Instruction | Operation | Cycles |
|---|---|---|
| `XCH A, Rn` | A ↔ Rn | 1 |
| `XCH A, direct` | A ↔ (direct) | 1 |
| `XCHD A, @Ri` | Lower nibble swap: A[3:0] ↔ (Ri)[3:0] | 1 |
| `SWAP A` | A[7:4] ↔ A[3:0] | 1 |

### E. Stack Operations
| Instruction | Operation |
|---|---|
| `PUSH direct` | SP ← SP + 1; (SP) ← (direct) |
| `POP direct` | (direct) ← (SP); SP ← SP − 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`XCH` vs `XCHD`:** `XCH` exchanges the *entire byte*; `XCHD` exchanges *only the lower 4-bit nibble*, leaving the upper nibble untouched. This is a frequent KTU viva question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table summarises **all** calculation formulas required to solve any numerical on this experiment.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine Cycle Period | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds |
| 2 | Oscillator Period | $T_{osc} = \dfrac{1}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds |
| 3 | Total Execution Time of a program | $T_{total} = T_{MC} \times \sum (\text{cycles per instr} \times \text{executions})$ | seconds |
| 4 | Memory Size of a region | $N = 2^{n}$ where $n$ = address bus width | bytes |
| 5 | Block transfer loop count | $N = \text{Final} - \text{Initial} + 1$ | bytes |
| 6 | Stack depth after PUSH k items | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} + k$ | bytes |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bar in formulas such as $|x|$ is rendered here as `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DMA-Style Buffer Management:** Moving ADC samples from one RAM buffer to another while the CPU prepares the next acquisition.
- **Communication Stacks:** UART receive/transmit ring buffers in protocols like Modbus or CAN.
- **Display Drivers:** Repacking 8-pixel columns into frame-buffer bytes for LCDs/LED matrices.
- **EEPROM Emulation:** Swapping active data pages in flash-based storage to balance write cycles (wear-levelling).
- **Cryptographic S-Boxes:** Table look-ups using `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` for AES/Substitution operations.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section is the **KTU board-exam core**: every program is dissected with exhaustive cycle counts, memory maps, and trace tables.

## 3.1 Program 1 — Single-Byte Transfer (Internal RAM)

**Aim:** Copy the contents of internal RAM location `30H` into location `50H`.

### Algorithm (Step-by-step logic)
1. Load the accumulator with the source byte using `MOV A, 30H`.
2. Store the accumulator into the destination using `MOV 50H, A`.

### Assembly Code

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
START:  MOV  A, 30H      ; A ← (30H)
        MOV  50H, A      ; (50H) ← A
        END
```

### Exhaustive Trace Table

Assume $(30H) = A5H$ initially.

| Step | Instruction | Bytes | Machine Cycles | A (Hex) | (30H) | (50H) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV A, 30H` | 2 | 1 | `A5` | `A5` | `xx` |
| 2 | `MOV 50H, A` | 2 | 1 | `A5` | `A5` | `A5` |

### Execution Time Calculation

Total machine cycles $= 1 + 1 = 2$.

$$
T_{total} = 2 \times T_{MC} = 2 \times \dfrac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_{total} = 2 \times 1.085 \, \mu s = 2.17 \, \mu s
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **single-byte transfer** is not the typical exam problem — KTU almost always asks for a **block transfer** with a loop. Program 2 below is the high-yield variant.

---

## 3.2 Program 2 — Block Transfer of N Bytes (THE KTU Classic)

**Aim:** Transfer 10 bytes of data from internal RAM locations starting at `30H` to locations starting at `50H`.

### Conceptual Strategy

A block transfer requires three pointers and a counter:

- `R0` → **Source pointer** (initialised to `30H`)
- `R1` → **Destination pointer** (initialised to `50H`)
- `R2` → **Counter** (initialised to `0AH` = 10 decimal)

The loop body: *fetch → copy → increment pointers → decrement counter → branch if not zero*.

### Assembly Code

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV  R0, #30H      ; R0 = source pointer
        MOV  R1, #50H      ; R1 = destination pointer
        MOV  R2, #0AH      ; R2 = counter (10 bytes)
LOOP:   MOV  A, @R0        ; A = M[R0]    -- FETCH
        MOV  @R1, A        ; M[R1] = A    -- COPY
        INC  R0            ; source ptr++
        INC  R1            ; dest ptr++
        DJNZ R2, LOOP      ; counter--; if not zero → LOOP
        END
```

### Exhaustive Trace Table (Iteration by Iteration)

Initial memory map:
- $(30H..39H) = 11H, 22H, 33H, 44H, 55H, 66H, 77H, 88H, 99H, AAH$
- $(50H..59H) = FFH, FFH, FFH, FFH, FFH, FFH, FFH, FFH, FFH, FFH$ (sentinel values)

| Iter | Before-Iter State (R0,R1,R2) | `A` after `MOV A,@R0` | (R1) after write | After-Iter State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (30, 50, 0A) | `11` | (50H)←`11` | (31, 51, 09) |
| 2 | (31, 51, 09) | `22` | (51H)←`22` | (32, 52, 08) |
| 3 | (32, 52, 08) | `33` | (52H)←`33` | (33, 53, 07) |
| 4 | (33, 53, 07) | `44` | (53H)←`44` | (34, 54, 06) |
| 5 | (34, 54, 06) | `55` | (54H)←`55` | (35, 55, 05) |
| 6 | (35, 55, 05) | `66` | (55H)←`66` | (36, 56, 04) |
| 7 | (36, 56, 04) | `77` | (56H)←`77` | (37, 57, 03) |
| 8 | (37, 57, 03) | `88` | (57H)←`88` | (38, 58, 02) |
| 9 | (38, 58, 02) | `99` | (58H)←`99` | (39, 59, 01) |
| 10 | (39, 59, 01) | `AA` | (59H)←`AA` | (3A, 5A, 00 → exit) |

### Execution Time Derivation

Per iteration, the instructions inside the loop are:

| Instruction | Bytes | Cycles | Iterations |
|---|---|---|---|
| `MOV A, @R0` | 1 | 1 | N |
| `MOV @R1, A` | 1 | 1 | N |
| `INC R0` | 1 | 1 | N |
| `INC R1` | 1 | 1 | N |
| `DJNZ R2, LOOP` | 2 | 2 | N (last iter: only 2 cycles for branch) |

Pre-loop setup:

| Instruction | Cycles |
|---|---|
| `MOV R0, #30H` | 1 |
| `MOV R1, #50H` | 1 |
| `MOV R2, #0AH` | 1 |

$$
C_{setup} = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 \text{ cycles}
$$

$$
C_{loop} = (1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 2) \times N = 6N \text{ cycles}
$$

$$
C_{total} = C_{setup} + C_{loop} = 3 + 6N
$$

For $N = 10$:

$$
C_{total} = 3 + 6(10) = 63 \text{ machine cycles}
$$

Converting to time (crystal = 11.0592 MHz):

$$
T_{total} = 63 \times \dfrac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 63 \times 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

$$
T_{total} \approx 68.4 \, \mu s
$$

### Generalised Time Formula for Block Transfer

For block size $N$ bytes with setup overhead $S$ cycles and per-byte loop overhead $L$ cycles:

$$
\boxed{T_{total} = (S + L \cdot N) \times T_{MC} = (S + L \cdot N) \times \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Program 3 — Single-Byte Exchange using XCH

**Aim:** Atomically swap the contents of internal RAM locations `40H` and `41H`.

### Assembly Code

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV  A, 40H        ; A ← (40H) = say 5AH
        XCH  A, 41H        ; A ↔ (41H). Now A = (41H)=A3H; (41H)=5AH
        MOV  40H, A        ; (40H) ← A = A3H
        END
```

### Trace Table

| Step | Instruction | A | (40H) | (41H) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Initial | — | `xx` | `5A` | `A3` |
| 1 | `MOV A, 40H` | `5A` | `5A` | `A3` |
| 2 | `XCH A, 41H` | `A3` | `5A` | `5A` |
| 3 | `MOV 40H, A` | `A3` | `A3` | `5A` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why three instructions and not just `XCH`?** Because the 8051 has **no direct `XCH 40H, 41H`** form. You must use the accumulator as the swap buffer. This is a favourite KTU viva question.

---

## 3.4 Program 4 — Nibble Exchange using XCHD

**Aim:** Exchange only the lower nibbles of the accumulator and internal RAM location `30H`. Upper nibbles must remain unchanged.

### Conceptual Use-Case
BCD arithmetic — separating units and tens digits in decimal displays.

### Assembly Code

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV  A, #0F2H      ; A = 1111 0010
        MOV  30H, #35H     ; M[30] = 0011 0101
        XCHD A, @R0        ; assume R0 = 30H
        END
```

### Trace Table (Bit-level View)

| Time | A (binary) | (30H) (binary) | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial | `1111 0010` | `0011 0101` | — |
| After `MOV A,#0F2H` | `1111 0010` | `0011 0101` | Load |
| After `MOV 30H,#35H` | `1111 0010` | `0011 0101` | Load memory |
| After `XCHD A,@R0` | `1111 0101` | `0011 0010` | Lower nibbles swapped |

The upper nibbles (`1111` and `0011`) are preserved. This is the entire purpose of `XCHD`.

---

## 3.5 Program 5 — Exchange of a Block of 8 Bytes (KTU Favourite)

**Aim:** Swap the contents of two blocks of internal RAM — locations `30H..37H` ↔ `50H..57H`.

### Algorithm

Use two pointers and a counter; in the loop, perform an `XCH` operation rather than a one-way copy.

### Assembly Code

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV  R0, #30H      ; ptr1
        MOV  R1, #50H      ; ptr2
        MOV  R2, #08H      ; counter = 8
LOOP:   MOV  A, @R0        ; A = M[R0]
        XCH  A, @R1        ; A ↔ M[R1]
        MOV  @R0, A        ; M[R0] = A (originally M[R1])
        INC  R0
        INC  R1
        DJNZ R2, LOOP
        END
```

### Trace Table (First Three Iterations)

Assume `30H..37H = 11,22,33,44,55,66,77,88` and `50H..57H = A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6,A7,A8`.

| Iter | (R0,R1) start | A after `MOV A,@R0` | A after `XCH A,@R1` | Memory after |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (30,50) | `11` | `A1` | (30)←`A1`, (50)=`11` |
| 2 | (31,51) | `22` | `A2` | (31)←`A2`, (51)=`22` |
| 3 | (32,52) | `33` | `A3` | (32)←`A3`, (52)=`33` |

After 8 iterations: block at `30H..37H` contains `A1..A8`, and `50H..57H` contains `11..88`. ✓

### Cycle Count

Per iteration: `1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 = 7` cycles (note: `XCH A,@R1` is 1 cycle).

$$
C_{total} = 3 + 7 \times N = 3 + 7(8) = 59 \text{ cycles}
$$

$$
T_{total} = 59 \times 1.085 \, \mu s \approx 64.0 \, \mu s
$$

---

## 3.6 Program 6 — External RAM Transfer using MOVX

**Aim:** Transfer 5 bytes from external data memory starting at address `2000H` to address `3000H`.

### Assembly Code

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV  DPTR, #2000H  ; source base
        MOV  R1, #00H      ; low-byte pointer (page offset)
        MOV  R2, #05H      ; counter

READ:   MOVX A, @DPTR      ; read external[2000H + i]
        PUSH ACC           ; save byte on stack
        MOV  DPTR, #3000H
        MOVX A, @DPTR      ; dummy read to latch addr (optional)
        POP  ACC
        MOVX @DPTR, A      ; write
        INC  DPTR
        DJNZ R2, READ
        END
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In practice the 8051 **cannot read external RAM and write it back in a single MOVX chain without reloading DPTR**. The cleanest idiom is two separate loops — one to read into a buffer, one to write — or to use `MOVX A,@DPTR` and immediately store A in internal RAM via `MOV direct, A`, then reload DPTR with the destination address.

### Cleaner Two-Pass Implementation

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        ; Pass 1: Copy external 2000H..2004H → internal RAM 30H..34H
        MOV  DPTR, #2000H
        MOV  R0, #30H
        MOV  R2, #05H
LP1:    MOVX A, @DPTR
        MOV  @R0, A
        INC  DPTR
        INC  R0
        DJNZ R2, LP1

        ; Pass 2: Copy internal RAM 30H..34H → external 3000H..3004H
        MOV  DPTR, #3000H
        MOV  R0, #30H
        MOV  R2, #05H
LP2:    MOV  A, @R0
        MOVX @DPTR, A
        INC  DPTR
        INC  R0
        DJNZ R2, LP2
        END
```

This implementation is **bulletproof** and is what KTU expects on the answer script.
