# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the MOV, MOVX, and MOVC instructions of 8051. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Instruction | Memory Space Accessed | Addressing | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `MOV` | Internal RAM & SFRs | Any of 5 modes | `MOV A, 30H` |
| `MOVX` | External Data Memory (XDATA) | Only indirect (`@DPTR` or `@Ri`) | `MOVX A, @DPTR` |
| `MOVC` | Program Memory (CODE) — read only | Only indirect (`@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC`) | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` |

The differentiation is based on (1) which physical memory is enabled by the control logic (`MOV` enables internal RAM via `RD*`; `MOVX` enables external RAM via `RD*/WR*`; `MOVC` enables code memory via `PSEN*`) and (2) which addressing modes are permitted.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for memory space, 1 mark for example.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Explain the `XCH` and `XCHD` instructions of 8051 with examples. State any one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

- `XCH A, byte` — Atomically swaps the contents of the Accumulator with the specified byte operand. *Application:* swapping two variables in a sorting algorithm (e.g., bubble-sort swap).
- `XCHD A, @Ri` — Swaps **only the lower 4 bits (lower nibble)** of the Accumulator with the lower nibble of the internal RAM location pointed to by `Ri`. Upper nibbles are unaffected. *Application:* BCD digit manipulation (separating tens and units digit).

*Example:* `XCH A, 40H` — exchanges `A` with the byte at `40H`. `XCHD A, @R0` (with `R0=30H`) — exchanges lower nibble of A with lower nibble of `30H`.

*(1.5 marks each instruction + application.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### INTERNAL CHOICE — Select EITHER Question A OR Question B

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** Write an 8051 Assembly Language Program to transfer **16 bytes of data** from internal RAM locations starting at `40H` to internal RAM locations starting at `60H`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State the addressing modes used in each instruction of your program. Calculate the **total execution time** if the crystal frequency is **12 MHz**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
START:  MOV  R0, #40H        ; source ptr  → block 40H..4FH
        MOV  R1, #60H        ; dest ptr    → block 60H..6FH
        MOV  R2, #10H        ; counter = 16 decimal
LOOP:   MOV  A, @R0          ; A = M[R0]
        MOV  @R1, A          ; M[R1] = A
        INC  R0              ; R0++
        INC  R1              ; R1++
        DJNZ R2, LOOP        ; R2-- ; branch if not zero
HERE:   SJMP HERE            ; infinite loop (trap)
        END
```

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Correct pointer initialisation: 2 marks
- Correct loop body with MOV indirect: 2 marks
- Correct counter management (`DJNZ`): 2 marks
- SJMP trap and END directive: 1 mark

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Addressing Modes Table:**

| Instruction | Addressing Mode |
|---|---|
| `MOV R0, #40H` | Immediate |
| `MOV R1, #60H` | Immediate |
| `MOV R2, #10H` | Immediate |
| `MOV A, @R0` | Register Indirect |
| `MOV @R1, A` | Register Indirect |
| `INC R0` | Register |
| `INC R1` | Register |
| `DJNZ R2, LOOP` | Register Direct |
| `SJMP HERE` | Relative |

**Execution Time Calculation:**

Cycle count:

- Setup: `MOV R0,#40H` (1) + `MOV R1,#60H` (1) + `MOV R2,#10H` (1) = **3 cycles**
- Loop body: `MOV A,@R0` (1) + `MOV @R1,A` (1) + `INC R0` (1) + `INC R1` (1) + `DJNZ R2,LOOP` (2) = **6 cycles per iteration**
- Trap: `SJMP HERE` (2 cycles, executed once)
- Final DJNZ exits when R2 = 0 — DJNZ takes **2 cycles even on the exit iteration** (the branch is not taken, but the cycle count is still 2).

$$
C_{total} = 3 + (6 \times 16) + 2 = 3 + 96 + 2 = 101 \text{ machine cycles}
$$

With $f_{osc} = 12 \, MHz$:

$$
T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1.0 \, \mu s
$$

$$
T_{total} = 101 \times 1.0 \, \mu s = 101 \, \mu s
$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Write an 8051 ALP to **exchange** the contents of two blocks of 8 bytes each, located at internal RAM `30H–37H` and `50H–57H`, using the `XCH` instruction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Trace the program for the first three iterations assuming initial contents `30H..37H = 01H,02H,03H,04H,05H,06H,07H,08H` and `50H..57H = A1H,A2H,A3H,A4H,A5H,A6H,A7H,A8H`. Also calculate the program size in **bytes**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV  R0, #30H        ; ptr to first block
        MOV  R1, #50H        ; ptr to second block
        MOV  R2, #08H        ; counter
LOOP:   MOV  A, @R0          ; A = M[R0]
        XCH  A, @R1          ; A ↔ M[R1]
        MOV  @R0, A          ; store back into M[R0]
        INC  R0
        INC  R1
        DJNZ R2, LOOP
DONE:   SJMP DONE
        END
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Trace Table for First 3 Iterations:**

| Iter | R0 | R1 | R2 | A after `MOV A,@R0` | A after `XCH A,@R1` | After `MOV @R0,A` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 | 50 | 08 | `01` | `A1` | (30H)←`A1`, (50H)=`01` |
| 2 | 31 | 51 | 07 | `02` | `A2` | (31H)←`A2`, (51H)=`02` |
| 3 | 32 | 52 | 06 | `03` | `A3` | (32H)←`A3`, (52H)=`03` |

**Program Size Calculation:**

| Instruction | Bytes |
|---|---|
| `MOV R0, #30H` | 2 |
| `MOV R1, #50H` | 2 |
| `MOV R2, #08H` | 2 |
| `MOV A, @R0` | 1 |
| `XCH A, @R1` | 1 |
| `MOV @R0, A` | 1 |
| `INC R0` | 1 |
| `INC R1` | 1 |
| `DJNZ R2, LOOP` | 2 |
| `SJMP DONE` | 2 |
| **Total** | **15 bytes** |

Code memory footprint = **15 bytes** (does not include the `ORG` and `END` directives, which are assembler directives only).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on this experiment:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `MOV` and `MOVX`.** A `MOV` to/from addresses ≥ 80H only works if the address happens to be an SFR. For genuine external RAM, **MUST** use `MOVX`. Wrong instruction → 0 marks for that line.
> 2. **Forgetting `DJNZ` consumes 2 cycles, not 1.** Several students write "5 cycles per iteration" instead of 6 — losing the entire timing sub-part.
> 3. **Using `MOV A, 30H` (direct) instead of `MOV A, @R0` (indirect) inside the loop.** This **breaks the loop** — you would need to increment A! KTU examiners treat this as a fundamental misunderstanding and award 0.
> 4. **Not initialising the stack pointer (SP).** If the program uses `PUSH/POP`, the SP defaults to `07H`, which overlaps with Register Bank 0. This corrupts registers. Always start with `MOV SP, #60H` (or similar).
> 5. **Exchanging without an intermediate register.** Remember that the 8051 has no `XCH direct, direct` — the accumulator is mandatory.
> 6. **Crystal-frequency unit errors.** KTU frequently uses `11.0592 MHz` — converting incorrectly gives a wrong execution time. Always write $T_{MC} = 12/f_{osc}$ first.
> 7. **Not declaring `END` directive.** Assembler errors out. Mark deduction in viva.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print & Carry)**
>
> - [ ] **Three address spaces:** Internal RAM, SFR, External XDATA, Code — matched by `MOV`, `MOVX`, `MOVC` respectively.
> - [ ] **`MOV` allows 5 addressing modes** (immediate, register, direct, register-indirect, base+index not in 8051).
> - [ ] **`MOVX` & `MOVC` allow ONLY indirect addressing** (`@DPTR`, `@Ri` for MOVX; `@A+DPTR`, `@A+PC` for MOVC).
> - [ ] **`XCH`** swaps entire byte via accumulator; **`XCHD`** swaps only lower nibble.
> - [ ] **`SWAP A`** swaps upper and lower nibbles of the accumulator in **1 cycle** — useful in BCD-to-binary conversion.
> - [ ] Block transfer pattern: **init R0, R1, R2 → loop body (MOV A,@R0 / MOV @R1,A / INC R0 / INC R1 / DJNZ R2,LOOP)**.
> - [ ] Block exchange pattern: same as transfer but with `XCH A, @R1` + `MOV @R0, A` sequence.
> - [ ] Stack operations: `PUSH direct` increments SP first; `POP direct` decrements SP after read.
> - [ ] **Crystal $11.0592 \, MHz$** → $T_{MC} = 1.085 \, \mu s$ (memorise); $12 \, MHz$ → $T_{MC} = 1.0 \, \mu s$.
> - [ ] Total program cycles = $\sum (cycles\_per\_instruction \times loop\_iterations) + setup$.
> - [ ] Always initialise **SP** before any `PUSH/POP` use.
> - [ ] **Default register bank** is Bank 0 (addresses `00H–07H`); switch via `RS0/RS1` bits in PSW.
> - [ ] **Bit-addressable region** is `20H–2FH` (128 bits) — accessed via instructions like `SETB`, `CLR`.
> - [ ] **Keil µVision workflow:** `.asm` → A51 assembler → `.LST` listing → `.HEX` → burn to AT89C51 via programmer / simulate in Proteus.
> - [ ] **Code memory is read-only via MOVC** — you cannot write to it at run-time.
> - [ ] **External RAM timing:** Each `MOVX` consumes **2 machine cycles** because of the extra bus cycles needed to latch the address and read/write.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Largest/smallest from a series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL508 - MICROCONTROLLER LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Largest/smallest from a series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **PCMRL508 – Microcontroller Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the experiment *"Largest/Smallest from a Series"* is a fundamental data-processing routine implemented on an 8-bit or 32-bit microcontroller (typically the **Intel 8051 family** or **ARM Cortex-M0/M3**). The objective is to scan a sequential block of data bytes/words stored in the **Internal RAM** (or code ROM) of the microcontroller, perform repeated **conditional comparisons**, and extract the **maximum (largest)** and **minimum (smallest)** numerical values from that data set.

The operation can be mathematically expressed as:

> Given a finite series $S = \{x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots, x_n\}$ stored in consecutive memory locations, determine:
> $$\text{Max}(S) = \max(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$$
> $$\text{Min}(S) = \min(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** This experiment tests **CO1 – Programming proficiency** and **CO2 – Interface peripheral understanding**. The student is expected to write the algorithm in either **8051 Assembly Language** or **Embedded C (Keil µVision IDE)** and verify the output using the **Keil Simulator** or hardware kits such as the **Nuvoton/Philips 89V51RD2** development board.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tallest Student in Class"

Imagine you are a class teacher standing at the door with a list of 30 students' heights written on a paper, and you must find the **tallest** and the **shortest** student.

- You start by assuming the **first student** is both the tallest and the shortest (this is your *initial reference*).
- You then move to the second student. You **compare** their height with your current reference.
- If the second student is **taller** than your current "tallest", you replace the tallest with them.
- If the second student is **shorter** than your current "shortest", you replace the shortest with them.
- You repeat this **pairwise comparison** for every student.

In the microcontroller world, the "students" are **bytes in memory**, the "height" is the **numerical value** of the byte, and the act of "comparing and replacing" is performed by the **Central Processing Unit (ALU)** using instructions like `CJNE`, `MOV`, or in C, simple `if-else` constructs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this experiment critical in a Microcontroller Lab?**
> It reinforces three foundational concepts: **(1)** Indexed/pointer-based memory access, **(2)** Loop-based iteration using counters, and **(3)** Conditional branching — all of which form the bedrock of every embedded system from a washing machine controller to an aircraft autopilot.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Definitions

| Term | Meaning in Microcontroller Context |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Series Length ($n$)** | Number of data elements (bytes) to be processed. |
| **Base Address** | The starting RAM/ROM location of the series (e.g., `30H`). |
| **Counter** | A register (R0–R7) tracking how many elements have been processed. |
| **Max Register** | Accumulator (`A`) or a dedicated memory location holding the current maximum. |
| **Min Register** | A general-purpose register holding the current minimum. |
| **Sign Flag (S)** | The PSW register's bit used to determine `greater` vs `less`. |
| **Carry Flag (C)** | Used in 8051 to detect unsigned underflow during decrement. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — The "Why" Behind Each Step

The algorithm for finding the largest/smallest element is a classic application of the **linear search** technique. It operates in **O(n) time complexity**, meaning the microcontroller performs exactly $n-1$ comparisons for a series of length $n$.

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown:

1. **Initialize the Pointer:** Load the **Data Pointer (`DPTR`)** or a register (`R0`/`R1`) with the **base address** of the series stored in memory.

2. **Load the Count:** Move the **series length ($n$)** into a counter register. This register acts as the "stopwatch" for the loop.

3. **Seed the Max and Min:** Read the **first element** from the series and store it in **two separate registers** — one designated as `MAX` and the other as `MIN`. This is a critical initialization; failing to do so results in incorrect output.

4. **Enter the Comparison Loop:** Increment the pointer to point to the second element. Fetch this element into the **Accumulator (`A`)**.

5. **Perform Conditional Compare:**
   - Use the `CJNE` (Compare and Jump if Not Equal) instruction or `SUBB` to subtract the current value from `MAX`.
   - If the result indicates the current value is greater, **update `MAX`** with this new value.
   - Similarly, compare the current value with `MIN` and update if smaller.

6. **Decrement and Loop:** Decrement the counter. If the counter has not reached zero, branch back to step 4. If it has reached zero, exit the loop.

7. **Store the Result:** The final values of `MAX` and `MIN` are stored in designated memory locations (e.g., `40H` and `41H`) for the user to inspect.

## 2.2 The Core Comparison Logic — Decoded

In **8051 Assembly**, the comparison essentially boils down to this:

```
CLR  C        ; Clear the carry flag before subtraction
MOV  A, MAX   ; Load current MAX into accumulator
SUBB A, @R0   ; Subtract current series element (with borrow)
JC   UPDATE   ; If Carry=1, it means A < @R0, so jump to update
```

The **Carry Flag (CY)** in the PSW (Program Status Word) register acts as the decision-maker. After a `SUBB` operation:
- If **CY = 1** → Accumulator was **less than** the operand → Current element is **larger** than MAX.
- If **CY = 0** → Accumulator was **greater than or equal to** the operand → No update needed.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Logic | Mathematical Form | 8051 Implementation | Real-Time Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Maximum Extraction** | $\text{MAX} = \max_{i=1}^{n}(x_i)$ | `CJNE` + `JC`/`JNC` | Sensor peak detection |
| **Minimum Extraction** | $\text{MIN} = \min_{i=1}^{n}(x_i)$ | `CJNE` + `JC`/`JNC` | Battery low-voltage alarm |
| **Time Complexity** | $T(n) = n - 1$ comparisons | N (loop counter) | Determines MCU speed requirements |
| **Memory Required** | $M = n + 2$ bytes | `DS` directive in ASM | RAM budgeting in embedded design |
| **Pointer Arithmetic** | $\text{Addr}_{i+1} = \text{Addr}_i + 1$ | `INC R0` or `INC DPTR` | Array traversal |
| **Termination Condition** | $\text{Counter} = 0$ | `DJNZ R2, LOOP` | Loop control |
| **PSW Sign Flag** | $\text{Result} < 0 \Rightarrow S=1$ | `JB PSW.7, label` | Signed comparison |
| **PSW Carry Flag** | $\text{Borrow} \Rightarrow C=1$ | `JNC label` / `JC label` | Unsigned comparison |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **`SUBB` instruction** is the most heavily tested instruction in this experiment. Examiners frequently ask: *"How does the 8051 determine whether a number is greater or smaller during comparison?"* — The answer is **always** via the **Carry Flag (CY)** after a `SUBB` operation, not the Accumulator value itself.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This "find largest/smallest" routine is not merely academic. It is the backbone of:

- **Peak Signal Detection** in ECG/Electrocardiogram monitors (finding R-wave peaks).
- **Battery Management Systems (BMS)** to detect the lowest cell voltage and trigger cell balancing.
- **Temperature Monitoring** in industrial furnaces — finding the maximum thermocouple reading for safety interlocks.
- **Voting Machine Logic** in electronic ballot systems (tallies and majority detection).
- **Data Acquisition Systems** for statistical analysis (min/max envelope tracking).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Trace of a 5-Element Series

Let us assume a series of **5 unsigned 8-bit numbers** stored in the **internal RAM starting at location `30H`**:

$$\text{Series } S = \{45, 12, 78, 33, 56\}$$

Stored in memory as:
| RAM Address | `30H` | `31H` | `32H` | `33H` | `34H` |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Value** | `45` | `12` | `78` | `33` | `56` |

The goal is to find $\text{MAX}(S) = 78$ and $\text{MIN}(S) = 12$.

### Iteration Trace Table

| Step | Counter (R2) | Pointer (R0) | Value at Pointer | MAX Register | MIN Register | Action |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Init** | `05H` | `30H` | — | — | — | Load base addr, count = 5 |
| **Seed** | `05H` | `30H` | `45` | `45` | `45` | Read first byte, seed both |
| **Loop 1** | `04H` | `31H` | `12` | `45` | `12` | 12 < 45 → Update MIN |
| **Loop 2** | `03H` | `32H` | `78` | `78` | `12` | 78 > 45 → Update MAX |
| **Loop 3** | `02H` | `33H` | `33` | `78` | `12` | 33 lies in range → No update |
| **Loop 4** | `01H` | `34H` | `56` | `78` | `12` | 56 lies in range → No update |
| **Exit** | `00H` | `35H` | — | `78` | `12` | Loop terminates, store results |

### Mathematical Justification of the Update Rule

At any iteration $i$, the decision to update `MAX` follows this logical gate:

$$
\text{UPDATE\_MAX} = \begin{cases} \text{True}, & \text{if } x_i > \text{MAX}_{\text{prev}} \\ \text{False}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Similarly, the `MIN` update rule is:

$$
\text{UPDATE\_MIN} = \begin{cases} \text{True}, & \text{if } x_i < \text{MIN}_{\text{prev}} \\ \text{False}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

In 8051 machine code, after the `SUBB A, @R0` instruction, the **Carry Flag** becomes the indicator:

$$
\text{CY} = 1 \iff A - M < 0 \iff A < M
$$

Therefore:
- For **MAX detection**: $\text{If CY=1, then } M > A \Rightarrow$ **Update** $A \leftarrow M$.
- For **MIN detection**: $\text{If CY=0, then } M \leq A \Rightarrow$ **Update** $A \leftarrow M$.

## 3.2 Full 8051 Assembly Program (Annotated)

```assembly
        ORG     0000H              ; Program starts at 0000H
        LJMP    MAIN               ; Jump to main program

        ORG     0030H              ; Data stored starting at 30H
SERIES: DB      45H, 0CH, 4EH, 21H, 38H   ; Series: 45, 12, 78, 33, 56

        ORG     0100H              ; Main program begins
MAIN:   MOV     DPTR, #SERIES      ; Load base address of series into DPTR
        MOV     R2, #05H           ; Counter = 5 (number of elements)
        MOV     R0, #30H           ; Pointer to data memory

        ; --- SEEDING PHASE ---
        MOV     A, @R0             ; A = first element (45H)
        MOV     MAX, A             ; MAX = 45H
        MOV     MIN, A             ; MIN = 45H
        INC     R0                 ; Point to second element
        DEC     R2                 ; Decrement counter (4 left)

LOOP:   MOV     A, @R0             ; A = current element

        ; --- CHECK FOR MAX ---
        CLR     C                  ; Clear carry flag
        MOV     B, A               ; Save current value in B
        SUBB    A, MAX             ; A = current - MAX
        JC      CHECK_MIN          ; If current < MAX, skip MAX update
        MOV     A, B               ; Restore current value
        MOV     MAX, A             ; Update MAX

CHECK_MIN:
        MOV     A, B               ; Restore current value
        CLR     C                  ; Clear carry flag
        SUBB    A, MIN             ; A = current - MIN
        JNC     CONTINUE           ; If current >= MIN, skip MIN update
        MOV     A, B               ; Restore current value
        MOV     MIN, A             ; Update MIN

CONTINUE:
        INC     R0                 ; Move pointer forward
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP           ; Decrement counter, loop if not zero

        ; --- STORE RESULTS ---
        MOV     40H, MAX           ; Store MAX in 40H
        MOV     41H, MIN           ; Store MIN in 41H

HERE:   SJMP    HERE               ; Infinite loop to halt

MAX:    EQU     50H                ; MAX stored at 50H (internal RAM)
MIN:    EQU     51H                ; MIN stored at 51H
        END
```

## 3.3 Equivalent Embedded C Version (Keil C51)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned char data series[5] = {0x45, 0x0C, 0x4E, 0x21, 0x38};
unsigned char max_val, min_val;

void find_largest_smallest(void) {
    unsigned char i;
    max_val = series[0];      // Seed MAX with first element
    min_val = series[0];      // Seed MIN with first element

    for (i = 1; i < 5; i++) {
        if (series[i] > max_val) {
            max_val = series[i];   // Update MAX
        }
        if (series[i] < min_val) {
            min_val = series[i];   // Update MIN
        }
    }
}

void main(void) {
    find_largest_smallest();
    while (1);   // Halt here — view max_val and min_val in Watch window
}
```

## 3.4 Verification of the Trace (End-to-End)

Let us manually verify the iteration with $x_3 = 78$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial MAX} &= 45 \\
\text{Current value} &= 78 \\
\text{Check: } 78 > 45 &\Rightarrow \text{True} \\
\text{Action: } \text{MAX} &\leftarrow 78 \\
\text{Initial MIN} &= 12 \\
\text{Check: } 78 < 12 &\Rightarrow \text{False} \\
\text{Action: } \text{MIN} &\text{ remains } 12
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the result $\text{MAX} = 78$ and $\text{MIN} = 12$, which matches our expected outcome. The algorithm is **mathematically correct** and **machine-verifiable** through the Keil µVision Debugger's **Watch Window** and **Memory Window**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START] --> B[Load base address into R0]
    B --> C[Load count N into R2]
    C --> D[Read first element into A]
    D --> E[Set MAX = A]
    E --> F[Set MIN = A]
    F --> G[Increment R0]
    G --> H[Decrement R2]
    H --> I{R2 = 0?}
    I -->|Yes| Z[Store MAX and MIN in memory]
    I -->|No| J[Read next element into A]
    J --> K{A > MAX?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Update MAX = A]
    K -->|No| M[A < MIN?]
    L --> M
    M -->|Yes| N[Update MIN = A]
    M -->|No| O[Increment R0]
    N --> O
    O --> P[DJNZ R2, Loop]
    P --> H
    Z --> Q[END - Infinite Loop]
    Q --> R[HALT]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — MCU System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU[8051 Microcontroller Core]
        ALU[Arithmetic Logic Unit]
        ACC[Accumulator A]
        PSW[Program Status Word - Flags]
        R0[Register R0 - Pointer]
        R2[Register R2 - Counter]
    end

    subgraph MEM[Internal RAM 128 Bytes]
        M30[Location 30H-34H: Series Data]
        M50[Location 50H: MAX Storage]
        M51[Location 51H: MIN Storage]
        M40[Location 40H-41H: Result Output]
    end

    subgraph PERI[Peripherals]
        DBG[Keil Debugger Watch Window]
        LED[Optional LED Display via Port P1]
    end

    ALU --> ACC
    ACC --> PSW
    PSW -->|Carry Flag feedback| ALU
    R0 -->|Address bus| M30
    R2 -->|Loop control| CPU
    M50 --> DBG
    M51 --> DBG
    M40 --> DBG
    M40 --> LED
```

## 4.3 Memory Layout Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph RAM["Internal RAM (00H - 7FH)"]
        R0R7["Register Banks 00H-1FH"]
        BS["Bit-Addressable 20H-2FH"]
        SF["Scratch/General 30H-34H: SERIES DATA"]
        MA["50H: MAX variable"]
        MI["51H: MIN variable"]
        RE["40H-41H: Final Result"]
    end
    R0R7 --- BS
    BS --- SF
    SF --- MA
    MA --- MI
    MI --- RE
```

## 4.4 Comparison Decision Tree (Logical View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Current Element X arrives in A] --> CmpMax{X > MAX?}
    CmpMax -->|Yes| UpdMax[Update MAX = X]
    CmpMax -->|No| CmpMin{X < MIN?}
    UpdMax --> CmpMin
    CmpMin -->|Yes| UpdMin[Update MIN = X]
    CmpMin -->|No| Skip[No update needed]
    UpdMin --> Next[Move to next element]
    Skip --> Next
    Next --> End{More elements?}
    End -->|Yes| Start
    End -->|No| Done[Output MAX and MIN]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: Write an 8051 ALP to find the largest number from a series of 10 bytes stored in internal RAM starting at location `40H`. Store the result in register `R7`.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     R0, #40H       ; Pointer to series (½ mark)
        MOV     R2, #0AH       ; Counter = 10 (½ mark)
        MOV     A, @R0         ; Load first element
        MOV     R7, A          ; Seed R7 = first element (1 mark)
        INC     R0
        DEC     R2
LOOP:   MOV     A, @R0
        CLR     C
        SUBB    A, R7          ; A = current - R7 (1 mark)
        JC      SKIP           ; If current < R7, skip
        MOV     A, @R0
        MOV     R7, A          ; Update R7
SKIP:   INC     R0
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP       ; (1 mark)
        END
```

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: How does the 8051 microcontroller differentiate between "greater than" and "less than" when comparing two unsigned numbers using the `CJNE` instruction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `CJNE` (Compare and Jump if Not Equal) instruction compares the first operand with the second by performing an **internal subtraction without affecting any register**. The result is evaluated as follows: **(1 mark)**

- If **operand A > operand B** → **Carry Flag (CY) = 0** → execution continues to the next sequential instruction. **(1 mark)**
- If **operand A < operand B** → **Carry Flag (CY) = 1** → a borrow is generated. **(1 mark)**

Therefore, by checking the state of the Carry Flag using `JC` (Jump if Carry) or `JNC` (Jump if Not Carry) after the comparison, the program can branch accordingly to update either the MAX or MIN register.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q: (a)** Design and write an 8051 Assembly Language Program to find both the **largest and smallest** numbers from an array of **8 unsigned bytes** stored in internal RAM locations `30H` to `37H`. Store the largest in `50H` and smallest in `51H`. Draw the algorithm flowchart. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Trace the algorithm step-by-step for the series $\{55, 23, 99, 14, 67, 88, 41, 32\}$. Show the final values of `MAX` and `MIN`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Algorithm Flowchart:** *(refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the verified flowchart)*

**Assembly Program:** (7 marks — distributed as 1 mark per logical block)

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     R0, #30H        ; 1. Pointer initialization (1 mark)
        MOV     R2, #08H        ; 2. Counter = 8 (1 mark)
        MOV     A, @R0          ; 3. Load first element
        MOV     50H, A          ; 4. Seed MAX (1 mark)
        MOV     51H, A          ; 5. Seed MIN (1 mark)
        INC     R0
        DEC     R2

LOOP:   MOV     A, @R0          ; Read current (1 mark)
        CLR     C
        SUBB    A, 50H          ; Compare with MAX
        JC      CHKMIN          ; If A < MAX, check MIN
        MOV     A, @R0
        MOV     50H, A          ; Update MAX (1 mark)
CHKMIN: MOV     A, @R0
        CLR     C
        SUBB    A, 51H          ; Compare with MIN
        JNC     CONT            ; If A >= MIN, continue
        MOV     A, @R0
        MOV     51H, A          ; Update MIN (1 mark)
CONT:   INC     R0
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP        ; Loop control
HERE:   SJMP    HERE
        END
```

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Trace Table for Series $\{55, 23, 99, 14, 67, 88, 41, 32\}$:** (7 marks — 1 mark for seeding + 6 marks for trace)

| Step | Element | Comparison with MAX (50H) | Action on MAX | Comparison with MIN (51H) | Action on MIN |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Seed | `55` | — | MAX = 55 | — | MIN = 55 |
| 1 | `23` | 23 < 55 | No change | 23 < 55 | MIN = 23 |
| 2 | `99` | 99 > 55 | **MAX = 99** | 99 > 23 | No change |
| 3 | `14` | 14 < 99 | No change | 14 < 23 | **MIN = 14** |
| 4 | `67` | 67 < 99 | No change | 67 > 14 | No change |
| 5 | `88` | 88 < 99 | No change | 88 > 14 | No change |
| 6 | `41` | 41 < 99 | No change | 41 > 14 | No change |
| 7 | `32` | 32 < 99 | No change | 32 > 14 | No change |

**Final Result:** $\text{MAX} = 99$ (stored at `50H`) and $\text{MIN} = 14$ (stored at `51H`).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the **role of the Carry Flag (CY)** in the 8051 PSW register during the comparison of two unsigned numbers. How is it used to update the `MAX` and `MIN` registers? Provide code snippets. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete **Embedded C program** for the 8051 (using Keil C51) to find the largest number from a 10-element array. Explain the role of the `data` memory qualifier. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

The **Program Status Word (PSW)** is an 8-bit special function register at address `D0H` in the 8051. The **Carry Flag (CY)**, located at **PSW.7**, is the most critical flag for unsigned comparison. **(1 mark)**

**Mechanism of CY during `SUBB`:** *(3 marks)*

The instruction `SUBB A, source` performs: $A \leftarrow A - \text{source} - C$. After execution:
- If $A < \text{source}$, a **borrow** is generated and **CY = 1**.
- If $A \geq \text{source}$, **CY = 0**.

**MAX Update Logic:** (1.5 marks)

```assembly
        CLR     C          ; Clear CY before compare
        MOV     B, A       ; Backup current value
        SUBB    A, MAX     ; CY=1 if MAX > current
        JC      NO_MAX     ; If current < MAX, don't update
        MOV     MAX, B     ; Else, update MAX
NO_MAX: ...
```

**MIN Update Logic:** (1.5 marks)

```assembly
        CLR     C
        MOV     B, A
        SUBB    A, MIN     ; CY=1 if MIN > current
        JNC     NO_MIN     ; If current >= MIN, don't update
        MOV     MIN, B     ; Else, update MIN
NO_MIN: ...
```

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned char code series[10] = {0x55, 0x23, 0x99, 0x14, 0x67, 
                                  0x88, 0x41, 0x32, 0x76, 0x05};
unsigned char max_val;

void main(void) {
    unsigned char i;
    max_val = series[0];                    // (1 mark) Seeding
    for (i = 1; i < 10; i++) {              // (2 marks) Loop
        if (series[i] > max_val) {          // (2 marks) Comparison
            max_val = series[i];            // (1 mark) Update
        }
    }
    while(1);  // Halt - inspect max_val in Watch window
}
```

**Role of `data` memory qualifier:** (1 mark)

The `data` qualifier in Keil C51 forces the variable to be stored in the **internal RAM (00H–7FH)** of the 8051, which provides the **fastest access time** (1 machine cycle). It is used for frequently accessed variables like loop counters and intermediate results. For larger arrays, the `xdata` qualifier (external RAM) is preferred, but it is slower (2 machine cycles).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to seed MAX and MIN** with the first element. A common mistake is initializing them to `00H` (zero), which is incorrect if the series contains only positive numbers and zero is not present.
> 2. **Confusing `CJNE` with `SUBB`.** The `CJNE` instruction **does not modify** the Accumulator — it only sets the Carry Flag. Students wrongly assume `A` is updated.
> 3. **Forgetting `CLR C` before `SUBB`.** The 8051's `SUBB` includes the carry flag in its subtraction. If CY is not cleared, the comparison becomes incorrect.
> 4. **Off-by-one errors in loop counters.** Initializing R2 to `N` but starting the loop from index 0 (which loads from R2=N) instead of starting after the seed. The correct pattern is: seed uses element 0, loop counter = N-1.
> 5. **Not decrementing the counter properly.** Forgetting `DEC R2` after seeding leads to one extra iteration.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ The algorithm requires exactly **$n-1$ comparisons** for a series of $n$ elements.
- ⭐ **Always seed** `MAX` and `MIN` with the **first element** of the series (element at index 0).
- ⭐ The **Carry Flag (CY)** in PSW is the sole decision-maker for unsigned comparisons in 8051.
- ⭐ **Always execute `CLR C`** before every `SUBB` instruction to ensure a clean comparison.
- ⭐ The loop counter is decremented using `DJNZ` (Decrement and Jump if Not Zero) — a single 8051 instruction that combines two operations.
- ⭐ The pointer is incremented using `INC R0` (for internal RAM) or `INC DPTR` (for code ROM lookup tables).
- ⭐ The **PSW.7 (CY)** bit can be tested using `JC` (Jump if Carry set) or `JNC` (Jump if Carry not set).
- ⭐ In **Embedded C**, the `data` qualifier places variables in the fast internal RAM; `xdata` places them in slower external RAM.
- ⭐ The **time complexity** is $O(n)$ — linear in the size of the series. This is optimal for an unsorted array.
- ⭐ The **space complexity** is $O(1)$ — only a constant number of extra registers are needed regardless of series size.
- ⭐ **Edge Cases to Test in Lab:** (1) All elements equal, (2) Sorted ascending series, (3) Sorted descending series, (4) Single-element series, (5) MAX/MIN at the first position, (6) MAX/MIN at the last position.
- ⭐ The Keil Debugger's **Watch Window** and **Memory Window @ 0x30 and 0x40** are the primary verification tools for this experiment.
- ⭐ Always include a **halt loop** (`HERE: SJMP HERE`) at the end of the program to prevent wild execution past the program boundary.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sorting (Ascending/Descending) of data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL508 - MICROCONTROLLER LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Sorting (Ascending/Descending) of data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **PCMRL508 – Microcontroller Lab**, **Sorting** refers to the algorithmic process of re-arranging a finite collection of data bytes (or words) stored in the **Internal RAM (IRAM)** of the target microcontroller (typically the **Intel 8051 family** in KTU laboratories) into a specified logical order — either **Ascending Order (smallest → largest)** or **Descending Order (largest → smallest)**.

The operation is performed **in-place**, meaning the original array buffer is overwritten as the sort progresses, without the use of external memory-mapped peripherals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim weightage):**
> *Sorting in a microcontroller environment is the manipulation of an array of N data elements stored in consecutive memory locations using nested looping constructs, conditional `CJNE` (Compare Jump Not Equal) instructions, and register-based swapping techniques to achieve a monotonic sequence.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hostel Room Cupboard" Intuition

Imagine you are a hostel warden and you have **10 unsorted attendance registers** lying on a shelf in random order. You want to arrange them by **roll number (smallest to largest)**.

**Two ways you could do this:**

| Approach | Real-World Action | Microcontroller Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Comparison Swap** (Bubble Sort) | Pick two adjacent registers, if they are in the wrong order, swap them. Repeat until shelf is fully sorted. | Compare adjacent memory locations via `CJNE` and swap using a temporary register (`A` / `R0`). |
| **Find-Min Place** (Selection Sort) | Scan the whole shelf, find the smallest register, place it in the first slot. Repeat for the next slot. | Find minimum in array, place at start, then repeat for remaining N-1 elements. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Sorting matters in Embedded Systems:**
> - Median filtering of sensor noise (removing outliers).
> - Lookup-table generation for fast DAC profiles.
> - Prioritizing tasks in a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS).
> - Ordered display on **16×2 LCD / 7-segment displays**.

### 1.3 Standard Parameters & Constants (Bolded for Examiner Emphasis)

- **Array Size (N):** typically limited to **≤ 255 elements** (single-byte index range of 8051).
- **Internal RAM:** **128 bytes** (Standard 8051) or **256 bytes** (8052 variant).
- **Swap Register:** **Accumulator (A)** acts as the universal temporary holder.
- **Index Registers:** **R0** (outer loop counter) and **R1** (inner loop counter).
- **Compare Constant:** Used with `CJNE` instruction — a single-byte comparison.

---

### 1.4 Types of Sorting Experiments in KTU Lab

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The three sorting variants frequently tested in the KTU 2024 Scheme External Lab Exam:**

1. **Ascending Sort** — smallest value appears at the lowest memory address.
2. **Descending Sort** — largest value appears at the lowest memory address.
3. **Signed / Unsigned Sort** — using `JB`/`JNB` on the **PSW.7 (Carry flag)** for signed magnitude comparison.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Algorithm Theory — The "Why" Behind Each Step

Sorting algorithms differ in **how** they bring order to chaos. For KTU 8051 lab exams, the **Bubble Sort** and **Selection Sort** dominate the syllabus because they map beautifully onto the 8051's register architecture.

#### **A. Bubble Sort (The "Adjacent Pair" Strategy)**

**Core Logic:** Repeatedly traverse the array; in each pass, the largest (or smallest, for ascending) element "bubbles up" to its correct final position.

**Why it works:** After pass `i`, the last `i` elements are guaranteed to be in their final sorted position — this is the **invariant** the examiner expects you to mention.

---

#### **B. Selection Sort (The "Find and Place" Strategy)**

**Core Logic:** For each position `i` in the array, scan the remaining unsorted portion `i+1` to `N-1`, locate the true minimum (ascending) or maximum (descending), and swap it into position `i`.

**Why it works:** After pass `i`, the first `i` elements are permanently fixed.

---

#### **C. Insertion Sort (The "Insert into Sorted Half" Strategy)**

**Core Logic:** Maintain a "sorted region" on the left; pick the next unsorted element and shift larger elements one slot to the right before inserting.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Preferred Answer:**
> Bubble Sort is most often asked because it maps directly to the **double-nested loop**, which is the key learning outcome of this experiment.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Symbol / Instruction | Value / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total comparisons (Worst Case) | $C_{max}$ | $\dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | dimensionless integer |
| Total swaps (Worst Case, Bubble) | $S_{max}$ | $\dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | dimensionless integer |
| Time Complexity (Worst) | $T(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | Big-O notation |
| Space Complexity | $S(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | In-place sort |
| Best Case (Already Sorted) | $T_{best}$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | Bubble sort only (with flag) |
| Pass count (Bubble, Ascending) | $P$ | $N - 1$ | ensures full sort |
| Memory addresses used | `30H` – `3FH` | example | Lower IRAM (0–1FH are register banks) |
| Index upper limit | $N_{max}$ | $255$ | single-byte unsigned |
| Swap instruction overhead | Cycles | $4$ machine cycles per `XCH` | 8051 @ 12 MHz clock |
| Carry flag interpretation | `CY` (PSW.7) | Set on borrow | used for unsigned compare |

> [!WARNING]
> When using the vertical bar symbol in any formula during derivations, always replace $\vert x \vert$ with $\lvert x \rvert$ or use `\mid` to prevent markdown table breakage.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of Sorting in Embedded Systems

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Industrial Automation** | Sorting sensor readings to discard outliers before PID computation. |
| **Medical Devices** | Median filter in ECG/EEG signal preprocessing (removes baseline wander). |
| **Automotive ECUs** | Prioritizing CAN-bus messages by ID (real-time scheduling). |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Alphabetizing contact lists on basic mobile phones. |
| **IoT Edge Nodes** | Sorting temperature samples to detect anomalies (e.g., fire alarm threshold). |
| **Robotics** | Sorting obstacle distances from ultrasonic array for nearest-target selection. |

---

### 2.4 Why 8051 is a Pedagogically Ideal Target

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 8051 architecture **forces** students to think at the **bit and byte level**:
> - No high-level array indexing — you must use pointer arithmetic with `@R0` / `@R1`.
> - No `for` loops — you must use `DJNZ` (Decrement Jump if Not Zero).
> - No built-in `swap()` — you must use the **`XCH`** instruction.
> - No `if-else` — you must use **`CJNE`** + conditional jumps.
>
> This is **exactly why KTU includes this experiment** — it builds low-level fluency.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Worked Example — Ascending Bubble Sort Trace

**Problem Setup (typical KTU lab question):**
> Sort the following 5 bytes stored in **internal RAM starting at address `30H`** in **Ascending Order**:
> `30H = 05H`, `31H = 02H`, `32H = 08H`, `33H = 01H`, `34H = 04H`

**Target Result:** `01H, 02H, 04H, 05H, 08H` at addresses `30H–34H`.

---

### 3.2 Algorithmic Step-by-Step Derivation

#### **Pass 1 (i = 0, compare j=0 with j=1, j=2, j=3):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compare } (30H, 31H) &= (05H, 02H) \rightarrow 05 > 02 \Rightarrow \textbf{SWAP} \\
\text{Array becomes:} \quad & [02, 05, 08, 01, 04] \\
\\
\text{Compare } (31H, 32H) &= (05H, 08H) \rightarrow 05 < 08 \Rightarrow \text{NO SWAP} \\
\text{Array remains:} \quad & [02, 05, 08, 01, 04] \\
\\
\text{Compare } (32H, 33H) &= (08H, 01H) \rightarrow 08 > 01 \Rightarrow \textbf{SWAP} \\
\text{Array becomes:} \quad & [02, 05, 01, 08, 04] \\
\\
\text{Compare } (33H, 34H) &= (08H, 04H) \rightarrow 08 > 04 \Rightarrow \textbf{SWAP} \\
\text{Array after Pass 1:} \quad & [02, 05, 01, 04, \boxed{08}]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Invariant satisfied:** Largest element `08H` has "bubbled" to position `34H` (last slot).

---

#### **Pass 2 (i = 1, compare j=0, j=1, j=2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compare } (30H, 31H) &= (02H, 05H) \rightarrow 02 < 05 \Rightarrow \text{NO SWAP} \\
\text{Compare } (31H, 32H) &= (05H, 01H) \rightarrow 05 > 01 \Rightarrow \textbf{SWAP} \\
\text{Array becomes:} \quad & [02, 01, 05, 04, 08] \\
\\
\text{Compare } (32H, 33H) &= (05H, 04H) \rightarrow 05 > 04 \Rightarrow \textbf{SWAP} \\
\text{Array after Pass 2:} \quad & [02, 01, 04, \boxed{05, 08}]
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Pass 3 (i = 2, compare j=0, j=1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compare } (30H, 31H) &= (02H, 01H) \rightarrow 02 > 01 \Rightarrow \textbf{SWAP} \\
\text{Array becomes:} \quad & [01, 02, 04, 05, 08] \\
\\
\text{Compare } (31H, 32H) &= (02H, 04H) \rightarrow 02 < 04 \Rightarrow \text{NO SWAP} \\
\text{Array after Pass 3:} \quad & [01, \boxed{02, 04, 05, 08}]
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Pass 4 (i = 3, compare j=0 only — last element is already smallest):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compare } (30H, 31H) &= (01H, 02H) \rightarrow 01 < 02 \Rightarrow \text{NO SWAP} \\
\text{Final Sorted Array:} \quad & [\boxed{01, 02, 04, 05, 08}]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Number of passes required = $N - 1 = 5 - 1 = 4$** ✓ matches our trace.
> **Total comparisons performed = $4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 10 = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$** ✓ matches formula.

---

### 3.3 Complete 8051 Assembly Program — Ascending Bubble Sort

```assembly
;=========================================================
; PROGRAM : ASCENDING BUBBLE SORT ON 8051
; AUTHOR  : KTU 2024 SCHEME STUDENT
; ARRAY   : INTERNAL RAM 30H TO 34H (N = 5)
; TARGET  : SORT IN ASCENDING ORDER (SMALLEST FIRST)
;=========================================================

        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:
        MOV     R0, #04H      ; OUTER LOOP COUNT = N-1 = 4
OUTER:
        MOV     R1, #04H      ; INNER LOOP COUNT INITIAL VALUE
        MOV     R2, #30H      ; R2 = BASE ADDRESS POINTER

INNER:
        MOV     A, @R2        ; A = ARRAY[J]
        INC     R2
        CJNE    A, @R2, CHECK ; COMPARE A WITH ARRAY[J+1]
        SJMP    NEXT          ; IF EQUAL, NO SWAP
CHECK:
        JNC     NEXT          ; IF A < ARRAY[J+1], NO SWAP
        ; ELSE SWAP
        XCH     A, @R2        ; A = ARRAY[J+1], @R2 = ARRAY[J]
        DEC     R2
        MOV     @R2, A        ; STORE NEW MIN INTO ARRAY[J]
        INC     R2
NEXT:
        DJNZ    R1, INNER     ; DECREMENT INNER COUNTER
        DJNZ    R0, OUTER     ; DECREMENT OUTER COUNTER

HERE:   SJMP    HERE          ; INFINITE LOOP (END)
        END
```

---

### 3.4 Instruction-by-Instruction Analytical Matrix

| Line | Instruction | Cycles | Bytes | Register State After | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV R0, #04H` | 12 | 2 | R0 = 04H | Initialize outer counter |
| 2 | `MOV R1, #04H` | 12 | 2 | R1 = 04H | Initialize inner counter |
| 3 | `MOV R2, #30H` | 12 | 2 | R2 = 30H | Point to array base |
| 4 | `MOV A, @R2` | 12 | 1 | A = (30H) | Load first operand |
| 5 | `INC R2` | 12 | 1 | R2 = 31H | Advance pointer |
| 6 | `CJNE A, @R2, CHECK` | 24 | 3 | CY set if A<(R2) | Compare and branch |
| 7 | `JNC NEXT` | 24 | 2 | — | Skip swap if A < next |
| 8 | `XCH A, @R2` | 12 | 1 | A ↔ (R2) | Atomic swap |
| 9 | `DJNZ R1, INNER` | 24 | 2 | R1–1, loop if ≠0 | Inner loop control |
| 10 | `DJNZ R0, OUTER` | 24 | 2 | R0–1, loop if ≠0 | Outer loop control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cycle Math:** Total machine cycles for $N=5$:
> $$
> T_{total} = (N-1) \times \Big[ \text{Overhead} + (N-i) \times \text{InnerCycles} \Big]
> $$
> For our case (no optimization flag): $T_{total} \approx 4 \times [12 + (5 \text{ to } 2) \times 100] \approx 1.4 \text{ ms}$ @ 12 MHz.

---

### 3.5 Descending Sort — Modification Logic

> [!NOTE]
> To convert the **ascending** program to **descending**, the comparison logic must be **inverted**:

```assembly
        CJNE    A, @R2, CHECK
        SJMP    NEXT
CHECK:
        JC      NEXT          ; CHANGE: JNC -> JC  (skip swap if A > next)
        ; ELSE SWAP (same as before)
        XCH     A, @R2
        DEC     R2
        MOV     @R2, A
        INC     R2
NEXT:
        DJNZ    R1, INNER
        DJNZ    R0, OUTER
```

**Logic table of conditional jumps:**

| Goal | Condition to SKIP swap | Jump Instruction |
|---|---|---|
| Ascending | A ≤ next element | `JNC` (no carry → A ≥ next, actually here **JNC** means A < next, so swap; check carefully) |
| Descending | A ≥ next element | `JC` (carry set → A < next, so swap) |

**Correct mapping (final, examiner-grade):**
- Ascending: swap when `A > @R2` → use `JNC` to **skip** swap (no carry means A ≥ @R2... careful!). The **standard textbook** pattern is:
  - `CJNE A,@R2,NEXT` ; if equal, no swap, jump to NEXT
  - `JC NOSWAP` ; if carry (A < @R2 in unsigned), don't swap → keep
  - swap otherwise

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always write the comparison-then-branch triplet as: **`CJNE` → `JNC/JC` → `XCH`** to score full marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 : STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Bubble Sort Algorithm (8051 Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([START]) --> initOuter["MOV R0, N MINUS 1"]
    initOuter --> initInner["MOV R1, N MINUS 1"]
    initInner --> ptrInit["MOV R2, BASE ADDRESS"]
    ptrInit --> loadA["MOV A, AT R2"]
    loadA --> incPtr["INC R2"]
    incPtr --> cmpInst["CJNE A, AT R2, BRANCH LABEL"]
    cmpInst -->|Equal| nextLabel["SJMP NEXT"]
    cmpInst -->|Not Equal| branchLabel["JNC NEXT OR JC NEXT"]
    branchLabel -->|Carry Set| swapStep["XCH A, AT R2"]
    branchLabel -->|No Carry| skipSwap["NOP"]
    swapStep --> storeBack["DEC R2, MOV AT R2, A, INC R2"]
    storeBack --> nextLabel
    skipSwap --> nextLabel
    nextLabel --> innerLoop["DJNZ R1, INNER BLOCK"]
    innerLoop -->|R1 Not Zero| loadA
    innerLoop -->|R1 Zero| outerLoop["DJNZ R0, OUTER BLOCK"]
    outerLoop -->|R0 Not Zero| initInner
    outerLoop -->|R0 Zero| endNode([SORT COMPLETE])
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Data Movement During Swap

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    memJ["MEM LOCATION J"] -->|Load via A| accum["ACCUMULATOR A"]
    accum -->|INC R2| memJ1["MEM LOCATION J PLUS 1"]
    memJ1 -->|CJNE Compare| compUnit["COMPARE LOGIC CJNE"]
    compUnit -->|Swap Needed| xchInst["XCH A, AT R2"]
    xchInst -->|Atomic Exchange| memJ1
    xchInst -->|Store back| memJ["MEM LOCATION J"]
    memJ -->|DEC R2 then INC R2| ptrMove["POINTER UPDATE"]
    ptrMove --> memJ
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Memory Layout Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph iram["INTERNAL RAM 8051"]
        bank0["00H TO 1FH REGISTER BANKS"]
        bitAddr["20H TO 2FH BIT ADDRESSABLE"]
        data30["30H = 05H ELEMENT 0"]
        data31["31H = 02H ELEMENT 1"]
        data32["32H = 08H ELEMENT 2"]
        data33["33H = 01H ELEMENT 3"]
        data34["34H = 04H ELEMENT 4"]
        sfr["80H ONWARDS SFRs"]
    end
    data30 --- data31
    data31 --- data32
    data32 --- data33
    data33 --- data34
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Pass-by-Pass Evolution Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pass0["INITIAL 05 02 08 01 04"] --> pass1["PASS 1 RESULT 02 05 01 04 08"]
    pass1 --> pass2["PASS 2 RESULT 02 01 04 05 08"]
    pass2 --> pass3["PASS 3 RESULT 01 02 04 05 08"]
    pass3 --> pass4["PASS 4 RESULT 01 02 04 05 08 SORTED"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Observe how the **rightmost elements become "locked"** after each pass — this is the core invariant examiners love to test.

---

### 4.5 Pin & Hardware Reference (8051 Kit Connection)

> Although this experiment is **purely software**, the KTU lab sheet may ask about I/O pins for display:

| Pin (8051 Port) | Function in this experiment |
|---|---|
| **P0.0 – P0.7** | Optional: drive LCD data bus to display sorted array |
| **P2.0 – P2.2** | Optional: LCD RS, RW, EN control lines |
| **P3.0 (RXD)** | UART output of sorted array (advanced variant) |
| **P1.0 – P1.3** | LEDs indicating sort completion state |

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module Lab Exam]**
**"Differentiate between Bubble Sort and Selection Sort. Which one performs fewer swaps in the worst case for an array of 8 elements?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Bubble Sort | Selection Sort |
|---|---|---|
| Strategy | Compare adjacent pairs | Find min, place at start |
| Worst-case swaps | $\dfrac{N(N-1)}{2} = 28$ | $N - 1 = 7$ |
| Worst-case comparisons | $\dfrac{N(N-1)}{2} = 28$ | $\dfrac{N(N-1)}{2} = 28$ |
| Stability | Stable | Unstable (in some implementations) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** **Selection Sort performs fewer swaps** (7 vs 28), making it preferable when swap cost is high — a common scenario in **EEPROM/Flash** write operations on embedded targets.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module Lab Exam]**
**"In the 8051 assembly program for ascending sort, what is the role of the `CJNE` instruction? Why can't we use a direct subtraction instead?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **`CJNE A, direct, rel`** (Compare Jump if Not Equal) performs the operation `$A - \text{direct}$` internally and sets the **Carry Flag (CY)** based on the result **without modifying the Accumulator A**. *(1 mark)*
2. This is critical because the Accumulator must be **preserved** for the subsequent `XCH` swap instruction. *(1 mark)*
3. Direct `SUBB` would destroy the original value of A, losing the data needed for the swap. `CJNE` is therefore **non-destructive** by design. *(1 mark)*

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Bubble Sort Full Implementation**

**[KTU University Exam – Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Write an 8051 assembly language program to sort an array of **10 bytes** stored in internal RAM from location `40H` to `49H` in **ascending order** using the **Bubble Sort** algorithm. Clearly explain the role of `R0`, `R1`, and `R2`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Trace the execution of your program for the input array `{07H, 03H, 09H, 01H, 06H}`. Show the array state after **every pass** and compute the total number of comparisons and swaps performed. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks:**

```assembly
; ASSEMBLY PROGRAM: ASCENDING BUBBLE SORT, N = 10
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:
        MOV     R0, #09H      ; R0 = N-1 = 9 (OUTER LOOP COUNTER)
OUTER:
        MOV     R1, #09H      ; R1 = N-1 (INNER LOOP COUNTER)
        MOV     R2, #40H      ; R2 = BASE ADDRESS OF ARRAY
INNER:
        MOV     A, @R2        ; A = ARRAY[J]
        INC     R2            ; POINT TO ARRAY[J+1]
        CJNE    A, @R2, NEXT1 ; COMPARE WITH NEIGHBOR
        SJMP    NEXT2         ; EQUAL: NO SWAP
NEXT1:
        JNC     NEXT2         ; A < NEXT: NO SWAP
        ; SWAP NEEDED
        XCH     A, @R2        ; SWAP A AND ARRAY[J+1]
        DEC     R2            ; POINT BACK TO ARRAY[J]
        MOV     @R2, A        ; STORE SMALLER VALUE
        INC     R2            ; RESTORE POINTER
NEXT2:
        DJNZ    R1, INNER     ; INNER LOOP
        DJNZ    R0, OUTER     ; OUTER LOOP

DONE:   SJMP    DONE
        END
```

**Register Roles (2 marks):**

| Register | Role | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **R0** | Outer loop counter | Holds `N-1`, decremented by `DJNZ` after each pass |
| **R1** | Inner loop counter | Tracks remaining comparisons in current pass |
| **R2** | Pointer (index register) | Holds address of current element `@R2` |

**Explanation of Logic (2 marks):**
- `CJNE` compares without destroying A — required for non-destructive swap.
- `XCH` performs atomic byte exchange between A and memory.
- The outer-inner nesting ensures all adjacent pairs are compared.

**Memory Notes (1 mark):**
- Array stored at `40H–49H` to avoid conflicting with register banks `00H–1FH` and bit-addressable region `20H–2FH`.
- Code placed at `ORG 0000H` (reset vector) with `LJMP MAIN` to skip interrupt vectors.

**Edge Cases (1 mark):**
- If array is already sorted, no swaps occur, but full $N-1$ passes still execute (this can be optimized using a **swap flag** in bit-addressable RAM).
- For $N = 1$, the outer counter is `0` and the program exits immediately — correctly handling the boundary.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Input Array:** `{07H, 03H, 09H, 01H, 06H}` at addresses `40H–44H`.

**Pass-by-Pass Trace (3 marks):**

| Pass | Comparison Pairs | Swaps | Array After Pass |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | (07,03)(07,09)(09,01)(09,06) | 3 | `[03, 07, 01, 06, 09]` |
| **1** | (03,07)(07,01)(07,06) | 2 | `[03, 01, 06, 07, 09]` |
| **2** | (03,01)(03,06) | 1 | `[01, 03, 06, 07, 09]` |
| **3** | (01,03) | 0 | `[01, 03, 06, 07, 09]` |
| **4** | (Already sorted — no work) | 0 | `[01, 03, 06, 07, 09]` |

**Numerical Computations (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Comparisons} &= (N-1) + (N-2) + (N-3) + (N-4) + (N-5) \\
&= 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 + 0 \\
&= 10 \\
&= \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2} = \dfrac{5 \times 4}{2} = 10 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Swaps (Actual)} &= 3 + 2 + 1 + 0 + 0 = 6 \\
\text{Worst-case swaps} &= \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2} = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Efficiency ratio** $= \dfrac{\text{Actual}}{\text{Worst-case}} = \dfrac{6}{10} = 60\%$.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Selection Sort Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – Alternative Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Write an 8051 assembly language program to sort an array of **8 bytes** stored in internal RAM from location `50H` to `57H` in **descending order** using the **Selection Sort** algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **time complexity** of Selection Sort with Bubble Sort. For which real-time embedded application would you prefer Selection Sort, and why? **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks:**

```assembly
; ASSEMBLY PROGRAM: DESCENDING SELECTION SORT, N = 8
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:
        MOV     R3, #07H      ; OUTER LOOP COUNTER = N-1
OUTER:
        MOV     R0, R3        ; COPY OUTER COUNT
        MOV     R2, #50H      ; BASE ADDRESS
        MOV     R4, @R2       ; R4 = CURRENT MAX CANDIDATE
        MOV     R5, R2        ; R5 = ADDRESS OF MAX
        INC     R2
INNER:
        MOV     A, @R2
        CJNE    A, 04H, CHK
        SJMP    NXTI
CHK:
        JC      NXTI          ; IF A < R4 (CARRY SET), SKIP
        ; ELSE A > R4, UPDATE MAX
        MOV     R4, A
        MOV     R5, R2
NXTI:
        INC     R2
        DJNZ    R0, INNER
        ; SWAP MAX INTO POSITION
        MOV     A, R5
        MOV     R1, #50H
        MOV     R6, @R1
        MOV     @R1, R4
        MOV     A, R5
        MOV     R1, A
        MOV     A, @R1
        MOV     @R1, R6
        DJNZ    R3, OUTER
DONE:   SJMP    DONE
        END
```

*(Note: A production-grade implementation uses additional pointer registers; the above demonstrates the **structural skeleton** required for full marks.)*

**Key Points for Marks:**
- Use of a **separate pointer R5** to track the address of the maximum (not just the value).
- Swap happens **only once per outer pass** — the defining characteristic of Selection Sort.
- Descending order means we **search for maximum**, not minimum.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Time Complexity Comparison (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{Bubble}}(n) &= \mathcal{O}(n^2) \quad \text{(both worst and average)} \\
T_{\text{Selection}}(n) &= \mathcal{O}(n^2) \quad \text{(both worst and average)} \\
\\
\text{Comparisons (both)} &= \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} \\
\text{Swaps: Bubble} &= \mathcal{O}(n^2) \\
\text{Swaps: Selection} &= \mathcal{O}(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

> Both share identical **comparison counts** — the key differentiator is the **swap count**, where Selection Sort wins by a factor of $n$.

**Real-Time Embedded Application Preference (3 marks):**

**Preferred scenario: EEPROM or Flash memory writes.**

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Write endurance** | EEPROM cells have limited write cycles (~100K). Selection Sort minimizes writes. |
| **Atomic write cost** | Each EEPROM write takes ~5 ms; reducing writes from $n^2$ to $n$ saves substantial time. |
| **Example application** | Sorting configuration parameters stored in EEPROM that are re-sorted periodically during firmware updates. |

**Bubble Sort is preferred when:**
- The array is **nearly sorted** (with swap-flag optimization, it becomes $\mathcal{O}(n)$).
- **Stability** is required (preserving relative order of equal elements).
- Code simplicity and code-size minimization are critical (smaller `.hex` file for OTA updates).

---

### **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost 2–4 marks each in this experiment:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to preserve Accumulator A** before `XCH`. Use a backup register or careful sequence.
> 2. **Using `SUBB` instead of `CJNE`** — destroys the data you need for swap. Examiner deducts 2 marks.
> 3. **Incorrect pointer restoration** after `INC R2` — forgetting the `DEC R2` before storing back breaks the sort.
> 4. **Confusing `JNC` and `JC`** when modifying between ascending and descending — read the comment table in Section 3.5 carefully.
> 5. **Placing the array in register-bank region** (`00H–1FH`) — collides with `R0–R7`. Always use `30H` onwards.
> 6. **Not mentioning the invariant** ("after pass i, last i elements are sorted") — loses a free conceptual mark.
> 7. **Writing `MOV A, R2` instead of `MOV A, @R2`** — pointer vs value confusion. Examiners check this aggressively.

---

### **TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Sorting (Ascending/Descending) of Data:**

- **Definition:** Sorting = reordering an array of N bytes in IRAM into monotonic sequence (ascending or descending).
- **Algorithm:** Bubble Sort (adjacent compare-swap, $N-1$ passes) and Selection Sort (find min/max, swap once per pass).
- **8051 Registers Used:** `R0` (outer), `R1` (inner), `R2` (pointer), `A` (accumulator + swap temp).
- **Key Instructions:** `CJNE` (compare, non-destructive), `XCH` (atomic swap), `DJNZ` (loop counter), `JNC`/`JC` (conditional skip).
- **Memory Region:** Use `30H–7FH` for arrays — avoid register banks (`00H–1FH`) and bit-addressable region (`20H–2FH`) unless intentionally using bit variables.
- **Ascending Logic:** `CJNE A,@R2,LABEL` → `JC NOSWAP` → `XCH` (swap if A > next). Wait — recheck: `CJNE` sets carry if $A < \text{direct}$. So if ascending wants to swap when $A > \text{direct}$, we **don't** want carry, hence `JNC SKIP_SWAP` and swap on the fall-through.
- **Descending Logic:** Reverse the conditional — swap when $A < \text{direct}$, so `JC SKIP_SWAP`.
- **Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ for both Bubble and Selection in worst case.
- **Space Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — in-place sort.
- **Best Case (Bubble with flag):** $\mathcal{O}(N)$ — already sorted input.
- **Total Comparisons:** $\dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ — independent of input data distribution.
- **Total Swaps:** Worst-case Bubble = $\dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$; Selection = $N-1$.
- **Invariant:** After pass $i$, the last $i$ elements of the array are in their final sorted position (Bubble Sort).
- **Stability:** Bubble Sort is stable; Selection Sort is unstable in its naive form.
- **Real-World Preference:** Choose **Selection Sort** for EEPROM writes (fewer swaps); choose **Bubble Sort** with flag for nearly-sorted data.
- **Examiner's Favorite Question:** "Modify the program to handle 16-bit data" — answer: use two consecutive memory locations and double the inner loop body.
- **Common Modification:** Add a swap-flag bit in `20H–2FH` region to break early on already-sorted input.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Addition, subtraction, multiplication, division of 8/16 bit data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL508 - MICROCONTROLLER LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Addition, subtraction, multiplication, division of 8/16 bit data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **8051 Microcontroller** (the standard KTU 2024 Scheme target architecture for the PCMRL508 lab), **arithmetic operations on 8/16-bit data** refer to the execution of the fundamental Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) instructions on data operands whose widths are either **8 bits (1 byte)** or **16 bits (2 bytes)**. These operations are performed using dedicated instruction mnemonics — `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `MUL`, and `DIV` — operating primarily on the **Accumulator (A)** and **B registers**, with results and flag status updated in the **Program Status Word (PSW)** register.

The key flag bits that govern the correctness and propagation of multi-byte arithmetic are:

- **CY (PSW.7)** — Carry Flag
- **AC (PSW.6)** — Auxiliary Carry Flag
- **OV (PSW.2)** — Overflow Flag
- **P (PSW.0)** — Parity Flag

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCMRL508, Module 2):** Students must be able to write, debug, and execute Assembly Language Programs (ALP) for the **addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division** of both **8-bit and 16-bit** numbers on the 8051 kit/Keil μVision simulator, and verify the result through memory inspection.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Calculator with Two Hands

Imagine a primary-school student doing arithmetic using a **simple pocket calculator** with only **two hands and a small blackboard**:

- One hand holds the **first number** (the Accumulator `A`).
- The other hand brings in the **second number** from memory (any register or direct address).
- The little **blackboard on the wall** displays a single small red light — the **Carry (CY) lamp** — that blinks if the addition exceeded the calculator's digit capacity.

For **8-bit arithmetic**, this student can only count up to **255 (0xFF)**. If the sum crosses 255, the carry lamp lights up, and the student must remember that overflow to handle the next higher digit. For **16-bit arithmetic**, two students work together — one manages the **lower 8 bits** and another manages the **upper 8 bits**, passing the carry lamp signal between them. This is precisely how 16-bit addition is implemented using the **`ADDC`** instruction.

For multiplication and division, the student has a special **multiply/divide box** (the **`B` register**), and the calculator takes one quick step to give a 16-bit product or an 8-bit quotient + remainder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** The 8051's ALU is fundamentally an **8-bit engine**. Any "16-bit operation" is a *programmer-constructed* sequence of 8-bit operations chained via the carry flag. This is the foundational concept that every KTU examiner tests.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Architectural Limits

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Accumulator (A) Width | **8 bits** |
| B Register Width | **8 bits** |
| Maximum Unsigned 8-bit Value | **255 (0xFF)** |
| Maximum Unsigned 16-bit Value | **65535 (0xFFFF)** |
| Internal RAM for data storage | **128 bytes (00H–7FH)** |
| Signed 8-bit Range | **−128 to +127** |
| Signed 16-bit Range | **−32768 to +32767** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Instruction Set Architecture for Arithmetic

The 8051 provides **five primary arithmetic instructions**. Each instruction operates on the Accumulator as the implicit destination and updates the relevant flags in the PSW.

#### 2.1.1 Addition Instructions

- **`ADD A, <src>`** — Adds the source operand (register, direct, indirect, or immediate) to `A` **without** including the carry flag.
- **`ADDC A, <src>`** — Adds the source operand to `A` **along with** the carry flag. Essential for multi-byte (16-bit) addition.

#### 2.1.2 Subtraction Instruction

- **`SUBB A, <src>`** — Subtracts the source operand and the **borrow (carry) flag** from `A`. The 8051 has **only one** subtraction instruction (no plain `SUB`); borrow handling is intrinsic. For the *first* byte of a multi-byte subtraction, the programmer **must clear CY** using `CLR C`.

#### 2.1.3 Multiplication Instruction

- **`MUL AB`** — Multiplies the unsigned values in `A` and `B`. The **8-bit lower byte** of the 16-bit product is placed in `A`, and the **8-bit upper byte** is placed in `B`. The **OV flag is set** if the product exceeds 255 (i.e., if `B ≠ 0`).

#### 2.1.4 Division Instruction

- **`DIV AB`** — Divides the unsigned value in `A` by the unsigned value in `B`. The **integer quotient** is placed in `A`, and the **remainder** is placed in `B`. The **OV flag is set** if division by zero is attempted.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow

**For 16-bit Addition (Op1 + Op2 = Result):**
1. Load low byte of Op1 into `A`.
2. `ADD` low byte of Op2 to `A` (this may set CY).
3. Store `A` into the low byte of Result.
4. Load high byte of Op1 into `A`.
5. `ADDC` high byte of Op2 to `A` (includes carry from step 2).
6. Store `A` into the high byte of Result.

**For 16-bit Subtraction (Minuend − Subtrahend = Result):**
1. `CLR C` (no borrow before LSB subtraction).
2. Load low byte of Minuend into `A`.
3. `SUBB` low byte of Subtrahend from `A`.
4. Store `A` into low byte of Result.
5. Load high byte of Minuend into `A`.
6. `SUBB` high byte of Subtrahend from `A` (borrow propagates automatically).
7. Store `A` into high byte of Result.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Instruction(s) Used | Flags Affected | Result Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8-bit Add | `ADD A, src` | CY, AC, OV, P | `A` |
| 16-bit Add | `ADD` + `ADDC` | CY, AC, OV | Two memory locations |
| 8-bit Sub | `CLR C` + `SUBB A, src` | CY, AC, OV, P | `A` |
| 16-bit Sub | `CLR C` + `SUBB` + `SUBB` | CY, AC, OV | Two memory locations |
| 8-bit Multiply | `MUL AB` | CY (always 0), OV, P | Low byte → `A`, High byte → `B` |
| 8-bit Divide | `DIV AB` | CY (always 0), OV, P | Quotient → `A`, Remainder → `B` |

#### Flag Calculation Formulas

$$
\text{CY}_{\text{addition}} = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } \text{bit}_7\text{ carry-out occurs} \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{AC}_{\text{addition}} = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } \text{bit}_3\text{ carry-out occurs} \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{OV}_{\text{signed}} = C_7 \oplus C_6
$$

Where $C_7$ is the carry into bit 7 and $C_6$ is the carry out of bit 7 (XOR detects signed overflow).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiplication & Division Quirk (Highly Tested!):**
> After `MUL AB`, if the product fits in 8 bits, the **OV flag is cleared** and **CY is always cleared**. After `DIV AB`, **CY is always cleared**, but **OV is set if the divisor (B) was zero** (undefined result scenario).

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

These fundamental arithmetic operations form the bedrock of all **embedded numerical computation**: digital weighing scales, temperature sensor data fusion, motor speed control via PID, digital signal processing (DSP) kernels like FIR filters, financial transaction terminal arithmetic, and IoT edge-computing nodes. Mastering 16-bit arithmetic is the gateway to working with 32-bit data types in advanced ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Programs

This section provides **fully worked-out 8051 Assembly Language Programs** for every arithmetic variant required in the lab. Each program is paired with an **execution trace** showing register/memory state evolution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** `Rn` denotes register R0–R7. `direct` denotes an internal RAM address (00H–7FH). `data` denotes an 8-bit immediate constant.

### 3.1 Program 1 — 8-bit Addition

**Objective:** Add two 8-bit numbers stored at internal RAM locations `30H` and `31H`. Store the 8-bit result at `32H`.

**Assembly Program:**
```assembly
        ORG 0000H          ; Reset vector
        MOV A, 30H         ; A ← [30H]   (first operand)
        ADD A, 31H         ; A ← A + [31H]  (second operand)
        MOV 32H, A         ; [32H] ← A   (store sum)
        END                ; End of program
```

**Execution Trace (Sample: [30H]=55H, [31H]=AAH):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad A &\leftarrow 55_{H} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad A &\leftarrow 55_{H} + AA_{H} \\
A &= 0101\,0101_2 + 1010\,1010_2 \\
A &= 1111\,1111_2 = FF_{H} \quad (\text{no carry}) \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad [32H] &\leftarrow FF_{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Execution Trace (Sample: [30H]=C3H, [31H]=7EH):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\leftarrow C3_{H} = 1100\,0011_2 \\
A &\leftarrow C3_{H} + 7E_{H} \\
A &= 1100\,0011_2 + 0111\,1110_2 \\
A &= 1\,0100\,0001_2 \\
\text{Low 8 bits: } &41_{H}, \quad \text{Carry-out (CY)} = 1 \\
\text{OV (signed): } &C_7 \oplus C_6 = 0 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
[32H] &\leftarrow 41_{H}, \quad \text{CY} = 1, \quad \text{AC} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Program 2 — 16-bit Addition

**Objective:** Add two 16-bit numbers. Op1 is at `30H–31H` (low–high), Op2 is at `32H–33H`. Store the 16-bit result at `34H–35H`.

**Assembly Program:**
```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV A, 30H         ; A ← Low byte of Op1
        ADD A, 32H         ; A ← Low(Op1) + Low(Op2)
        MOV 34H, A         ; Store low byte of sum
        MOV A, 31H         ; A ← High byte of Op1
        ADDC A, 33H        ; A ← High(Op1) + High(Op2) + CY
        MOV 35H, A         ; Store high byte of sum
        END
```

**Mathematical Derivation (Sample: Op1 = 1234H, Op2 = 5678H):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Op1} &= 1234_{H} = 0001\,0010\,0011\,0100_2 \\
\text{Op2} &= 5678_{H} = 0101\,0110\,0111\,1000_2 \\
\text{Sum} &= 1234_{H} + 5678_{H} = 68AC_{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-Step Execution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad A &\leftarrow 34_{H} \quad (\text{Low byte of Op1}) \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad A &\leftarrow 34_{H} + 78_{H} \\
A &= 0011\,0100_2 + 0111\,1000_2 \\
&= 1010\,1100_2 = AC_{H} \quad (\text{CY} = 0) \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad [34H] &\leftarrow AC_{H} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad A &\leftarrow 12_{H} \quad (\text{High byte of Op1}) \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad A &\leftarrow 12_{H} + 56_{H} + \text{CY}(0) \\
A &= 0001\,0010_2 + 0101\,0110_2 \\
&= 0110\,1000_2 = 68_{H} \quad (\text{CY} = 0) \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad [35H] &\leftarrow 68_{H} \\
\therefore \text{Result} &= 68AC_{H} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Program 3 — 8-bit Subtraction

**Objective:** Subtract [31H] from [30H]; store result at [32H].

**Assembly Program:**
```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        CLR C              ; Clear CY (no borrow before LSB)
        MOV A, 30H         ; A ← Minuend
        SUBB A, 31H        ; A ← A − [31H] − CY
        MOV 32H, A         ; Store result
        END
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `CLR C` is Mandatory:** The `SUBB` instruction **always subtracts the carry flag** in addition to the source operand. If CY is not cleared before the first subtraction in a chain, the result will be off by one.

**Execution Trace (Sample: [30H]=A5H, [31H]=3FH):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\leftarrow A5_{H} = 1010\,0101_2 \\
A &\leftarrow A5_{H} - 3F_{H} - 0 \\
A &= 1010\,0101_2 - 0011\,1111_2 \\
&= 0110\,0110_2 = 66_{H} \\
\text{CY} &= 0 \quad (\text{no borrow required}) \\
\text{AC} &= 0 \\
[32H] &\leftarrow 66_{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Execution Trace (Sample: [30H]=20H, [31H]=85H — borrow case):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\leftarrow 20_{H} = 0010\,0000_2 \\
A &\leftarrow 20_{H} - 85_{H} - 0 \\
A &= 0010\,0000_2 - 1000\,0101_2 \\
&= 1001\,1011_2 = 9B_{H} \quad (\text{2's complement of 65H}) \\
\text{CY} &= 1 \quad (\text{borrow required}) \\
[32H] &\leftarrow 9B_{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Program 4 — 16-bit Subtraction

**Objective:** Minuend at `40H–41H`, Subtrahend at `42H–43H`. Store result at `44H–45H`.

**Assembly Program:**
```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        CLR C              ; Clear CY
        MOV A, 40H         ; Low byte of Minuend
        SUBB A, 42H        ; A ← Low(M) − Low(S) − CY
        MOV 44H, A         ; Store low byte
        MOV A, 41H         ; High byte of Minuend
        SUBB A, 43H        ; A ← High(M) − High(S) − CY
        MOV 45H, A         ; Store high byte
        END
```

**Derivation (Sample: M = 8000H, S = 0001H):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LSB:} \quad A &\leftarrow 00_{H} - 01_{H} - 0 \\
&= FF_{H} \quad (\text{CY} = 1, \text{borrow}) \\
\text{MSB:} \quad A &\leftarrow 80_{H} - 00_{H} - 1 \\
&= 7F_{H} \quad (\text{CY} = 0) \\
\therefore \text{Result} &= 7FFF_{H} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Program 5 — 8-bit Multiplication

**Objective:** Multiply [30H] by [31H]; store 16-bit product at `32H` (low) and `33H` (high).

**Assembly Program:**
```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV A, 30H         ; A ← Multiplicand
        MOV B, 31H         ; B ← Multiplier
        MUL AB             ; B:A ← A × B
        MOV 32H, A         ; Store low byte of product
        MOV 33H, B         ; Store high byte of product
        END
```

**Derivation (Sample: 0F0H × 0AH):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\leftarrow F0_{H} = 240_{10} \\
B &\leftarrow 0A_{H} = 10_{10} \\
B:A &\leftarrow 240 \times 10 = 2400_{10} = 0960_{H} \\
A &\leftarrow 60_{H} \quad (\text{low byte}) \\
B &\leftarrow 09_{H} \quad (\text{high byte}) \\
[32H] &\leftarrow 60_{H}, \quad [33H] \leftarrow 09_{H} \\
\text{OV} &= 1 \quad (\text{product} > 255) \\
\text{CY} &= 0 \quad (\text{always for MUL})
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.6 Program 6 — 8-bit Division

**Objective:** Divide [30H] (dividend) by [31H] (divisor). Store quotient at `33H`, remainder at `32H`.

**Assembly Program:**
```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV A, 30H         ; A ← Dividend
        MOV B, 31H         ; B ← Divisor
        DIV AB             ; A ← Quotient, B ← Remainder
        MOV 33H, A         ; Store quotient
        MOV 32H, B         ; Store remainder
        END
```

**Derivation (Sample: FBH ÷ 12H):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\leftarrow FB_{H} = 251_{10} \\
B &\leftarrow 12_{H} = 18_{10} \\
\text{Quotient} &= \lfloor 251 / 18 \rfloor = 13_{10} = 0D_{H} \\
\text{Remainder} &= 251 - (13 \times 18) = 17_{10} = 11_{H} \\
A &\leftarrow 0D_{H}, \quad B \leftarrow 11_{H} \\
[33H] &\leftarrow 0D_{H}, \quad [32H] \leftarrow 11_{H} \\
\text{CY} &= 0, \quad \text{OV} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.7 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix

| Operation | No. of Instructions | Critical Flag | Common Bug |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8-bit Add | 3 | CY | Forgetting to clear CY is *not* needed for `ADD` |
| 16-bit Add | 5 | CY | Using `ADD` instead of `ADDC` for MSB |
| 8-bit Sub | 4 | CY | Forgetting `CLR C` before `SUBB` |
| 16-bit Sub | 6 | CY | Using `SUB` instead of `SUBB` |
| 8-bit Mul | 5 | OV | Swapping quotient/remainder location for `DIV` |
| 8-bit Div | 5 | OV | Division by zero leaves OV=1 |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Explain the function of the `MUL AB` instruction in the 8051 microcontroller. What happens to the CY and OV flags after its execution?

**Model Answer:**
The `MUL AB` instruction multiplies the unsigned 8-bit value in the Accumulator (A) with the unsigned 8-bit value in the B register. The resulting 16-bit product is stored with its **low byte in A** and its **high byte in B**. After execution, the **CY flag is always cleared (CY = 0)**, while the **OV flag is set (OV = 1) if the product exceeds 255** (i.e., if the high byte in B is non-zero); otherwise, OV is cleared. The parity flag (P) is updated based on the new value of A.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Why is it necessary to clear the carry flag using `CLR C` before performing a 16-bit subtraction on the 8051?

**Model Answer:**
The 8051 has only one subtraction instruction, `SUBB`, which **subtracts both the source operand AND the carry (borrow) flag** from the Accumulator. For the first (least significant) byte of a multi-byte subtraction, there is no prior borrow; hence the carry flag must be explicitly cleared via `CLR C`.
