Failing to do so would result in an erroneous off-by-one subtraction in the LSB step. For subsequent higher-order bytes, the carry flag automatically holds the borrow from the previous step, so `CLR C` is not required.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question A]**

#### **Question A**

**(a)** Write an 8051 Assembly Language Program to **add two 16-bit numbers** stored in internal RAM locations `30H–31H` and `32H–33H`. Store the 16-bit result in locations `34H–35H`. Explain the role of the carry flag in this operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8051 ALP to **multiply two 8-bit numbers** stored at RAM locations `40H` and `41H`. The 16-bit result should be stored at `42H–43H`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) 16-bit Addition — Complete Code:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV A, 30H         ; Step 1: Load LSB of Operand 1
        ADD A, 32H         ; Step 2: Add LSB of Operand 2
        MOV 34H, A         ; Step 3: Store LSB of result
        MOV A, 31H         ; Step 4: Load MSB of Operand 1
        ADDC A, 33H        ; Step 5: Add MSB + carry from LSB
        MOV 35H, A         ; Step 6: Store MSB of result
HERE:   SJMP HERE          ; Infinite loop to halt
        END
```

**Role of Carry Flag:**
The CY flag, set automatically by the `ADD` instruction in Step 2 if the LSB addition overflows beyond `FFH`, is **propagated** into the MSB addition in Step 5 via the `ADDC` instruction. Without this propagation, the 16-bit result would be incorrect whenever the lower byte sum exceeds 255.

**(b) 8-bit Multiplication — Complete Code:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV A, 40H         ; Load multiplicand
        MOV B, 41H         ; Load multiplier
        MUL AB             ; B:A = A × B (16-bit product)
        MOV 42H, A         ; Store LSB of product
        MOV 43H, B         ; Store MSB of product
HERE:   SJMP HERE
        END
```

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question B]**

#### **Question B**

**(a)** Write an 8051 ALP to **subtract two 16-bit numbers**, where the minuend is stored at `40H–41H` and the subtrahend at `42H–43H`. Store the result at `44H–45H`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8051 ALP to **divide two 8-bit numbers**, where the dividend is at `50H` and the divisor is at `51H`. Store the quotient at `52H` and the remainder at `53H`. Explain what happens if the divisor is zero. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) 16-bit Subtraction — Complete Code:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        CLR C              ; Step 1: Clear CY (no initial borrow)
        MOV A, 40H         ; Step 2: Load LSB of Minuend
        SUBB A, 42H        ; Step 3: LSB Result = LSB(M) − LSB(S) − CY
        MOV 44H, A         ; Step 4: Store LSB result
        MOV A, 41H         ; Step 5: Load MSB of Minuend
        SUBB A, 43H        ; Step 6: MSB Result = MSB(M) − MSB(S) − CY
        MOV 45H, A         ; Step 7: Store MSB result
HERE:   SJMP HERE
        END
```

**(b) 8-bit Division — Complete Code:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV A, 50H         ; Load dividend
        MOV B, 51H         ; Load divisor
        DIV AB             ; A = quotient, B = remainder
        MOV 52H, A         ; Store quotient
        MOV 53H, B         ; Store remainder
HERE:   SJMP HERE
        END
```

**Behavior on Division by Zero:**
If `B = 00H` (divisor is zero), the `DIV AB` instruction sets the **OV (overflow) flag to 1**, and the values in `A` and `B` are **undefined / indeterminate**. Program execution continues, but the programmer should check the OV flag after division to handle this error gracefully. The CY flag is always cleared after `DIV AB`, regardless of operand validity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 2–3 Marks Each):**
> 1. **Forgetting `CLR C`** before the first `SUBB` instruction → LSB result is wrong by 1.
> 2. **Using `ADD` instead of `ADDC`** for the high byte in 16-bit addition → CY propagation is lost; fails for sums > `00FFH`.
> 3. **Swapping A and B storage** after `MUL AB` — students often forget the high byte goes to B.
> 4. **Storing quotient to wrong memory location** after `DIV AB` — quotient goes to `A`, remainder to `B`.
> 5. **Not halting the program** with `SJMP HERE` or `END` properly → simulator shows garbage after the intended instructions.
> 6. **Mixing up 16-bit operand byte order** — always remember: low byte at the lower address, high byte at the higher address (little-endian convention in 8051).

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Five core instructions:** `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `MUL AB`, `DIV AB`.
- **ADD vs ADDC:** `ADD` ignores carry; `ADDC` includes carry. Use `ADDC` for higher-order bytes in multi-byte addition.
- **SUBB quirk:** Always subtracts the carry flag too. Use `CLR C` before the **first** `SUBB` in any multi-byte subtraction sequence.
- **MUL AB result location:** Low byte in **A**, high byte in **B**. OV flag indicates whether result > 255.
- **DIV AB result location:** Quotient in **A**, remainder in **B**. OV flag indicates division by zero error.
- **Carry (CY):** Set on unsigned overflow out of bit 7 (addition) or borrow required (subtraction).
- **Auxiliary Carry (AC):** Set on carry out of bit 3; critical for **BCD arithmetic** (DA A instruction).
- **Overflow (OV):** Set on signed overflow; computed as XOR of carry into and out of bit 7.
- **16-bit storage convention:** Low byte at lower address (e.g., `30H`), high byte at higher address (e.g., `31H`) — standard little-endian 8051 layout.
- **8051 MUL/DIV are 8-bit only:** No native 16-bit multiply/divide. Multi-byte versions must be implemented by the programmer using loops and partial products / repeated subtraction.
- **END directive:** Always end your ALP with `END` to signal the assembler that no more instructions follow.
- **Halting:** Use `HERE: SJMP HERE` (infinite loop) at the end of programs in Keil μVision to prevent execution from falling into garbage memory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sum of a series of 8-bit data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRL508 - MICROCONTROLLER LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Sum of a series of 8-bit data # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > *"Sum of a series of 8-bit data" is a fundamental 8051 Microcontroller assembly language experiment in which a block of **N** 8-bit (byte-sized) operands stored consecutively in the internal RAM (or external data memory) of the 8051 are arithmetically added using the **Accumulator (A)** as the primary working register, employing **indirect addressing** through **R0/R1** as the data pointer, and the result is stored back in memory as a 16-bit value (with the carry generated during the additions accounted for).* In strict KTU lab-manual terminology, this experiment demonstrates the practical implementation of: - Indirect addressing mode using `@R0` / `@R1`. - Iterative looping using the `DJNZ` (Decrement and Jump if Not Zero) instruction. - Arithmetic addition with implicit carry propagation through the **PSW (Program Status Word)** register, specifically the **Carry Flag (CY)** at bit address `D7` of `PSW (D0H)`. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket of Apples" Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Running-Total Analogy** > Imagine you are a cashier at a grocery store. You have a conveyor belt of apples, each with a price tag (an 8-bit number). You keep a **tally register** (Accumulator) in your hand. As each apple passes, you read its price and add it to your running total in your head. You use your **index finger** (R0) to point to the next apple's price tag, and you keep a **counter on the wall** (R1 or R2) that tells you how many apples are left. When the counter hits zero, you stop and write down your final total on paper (memory location). This simple analogy maps directly onto the 8051 program: - The **Accumulator (A)** = the tally register in your head. - The **pointer register (R0/R1)** = your pointing index finger. - The **loop counter** = the wall-mounted counter. - The **final memory location** = the paper you write on. ## 1.3 Key Terminology & Constants | Term | Value / Definition | | :--- | :--- | | **Accumulator (A)** | **8-bit primary arithmetic register**, address `E0H` in SFR space | | **PSW (Program Status Word)** | **8-bit status register** at address `D0H`; bit 7 = Carry Flag (CY) | | **R0–R7** | **8 general-purpose registers** in the active register bank | | **Indirect Address** | Address pointed to by `@R0` or `@R1` (only R0/R1 allowed) | | **Maximum 8-bit value** | **`255 (FFH)`** — any sum exceeding this generates a Carry | | **Typical Data Range** | **`00H` to `FFH`** (unsigned 8-bit) | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Concept — Why and How The 8051 is an **8-bit microcontroller**, meaning its core ALU operates natively on bytes. When a series of 8-bit numbers are summed: 1. The **Accumulator (A)** holds the running partial sum. 2. Each operand is fetched from memory using the **indirect addressing mode** (because the address is *not* a constant — it changes at every iteration). 3. The instruction `ADD A, @R0` performs the binary addition and **automatically sets the Carry Flag (CY)** in PSW if the result exceeds `0xFF`. 4. The loop is bounded by the **DJNZ** instruction, which decrements a register and jumps until it reaches zero. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why 16-bit storage is required for the final result** > If you sum **N = 10** bytes, the theoretical maximum is `10 × 255 = 2550 (09F6H)`. This **overflows a single byte**. The final result must therefore be stored across **two memory locations**: the low byte (sum ≤ FFH) and the high byte (carry accumulated). ## 2.2 The 8051 Instructions Used (KTU Formula Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Instruction Reference — Memorize this table before the exam.** | Mnemonic | Bytes | Machine Cycles | Operation | Why It Is Used Here | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | `MOV A, #data` | 2 | 1 | `A ← immediate` | Initialize accumulator to 00H | | `MOV R0, #addr` | 2 | 1 | `R0 ← source address` | Point to start of data array | | `MOV R1, #N` | 2 | 1 | `R1 ← count` | Load loop counter | | `MOVX A, @R0` | 1 | 2 | `A ← external[RO]` | Read byte (if external RAM) | | `ADD A, @R0` | 1 | 1 | `A ← A + mem[RO]` | **Core summation instruction** | | `INC R0` | 1 | 1 | `R0 ← R0 + 1` | Advance data pointer | | `DJNZ R1, label` | 2 | 2 | `R1--; if R1≠0 jump` | **Loop control** | | `MOV @R0, A` | 1 | 1 | `mem[RO] ← A` | Store partial / final result | | `JNC label` | 2 | 2 | Jump if `CY = 0` | Branch based on carry | | `CPL C` | 1 | 1 | `CY ← NOT CY` | Invert carry (for accumulation) | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Mathematical Foundation | Formula / Identity | Meaning | Use Case | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\text{Sum} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} D_i$ | Mathematical summation of N data bytes | Defines the final result | | $\text{Range of Sum} = [N, 255N]$ | Minimum and maximum possible sum | Determines if 16-bit result is needed | | $\text{High Byte} = \sum (\text{carries generated})$ | Each carry contributes 1 to the high byte | Algorithm refinement | | $\text{Low Byte} = A \pmod{256}$ | Final value of the accumulator | Always in `00H–FFH` range | | $N_{\max\_byte} = 256 \text{ operands}$ | Beyond this, high byte is guaranteed | Sanity check for N | | $\text{Final Result} = (256 \times \text{HiByte}) + \text{LoByte}$ | Recombining 16-bit result | Validation step | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Relevance** > The "sum of a series" operation is the kernel of: > - **Digital Signal Processing (DSP):** Moving average filters, FIR filter accumulation. > - **Sensor Data Aggregation:** Summing 8-bit ADC readings over a sampling window. > - **Checksum Computation:** Internet and memory checksums rely on byte-wise addition. > - **Statistical Computation:** Mean calculation by summation + division. > - **Embedded Arithmetic Units:** Co-processors in IoT edge devices. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Complete Algorithm (Mathematical Derivation) Let the data block contain $N$ bytes located at internal RAM addresses `30H` to `30H + N − 1`. $$ \text{Goal: } S = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} M(30H + i) $$ where $M(x)$ denotes the byte stored at address $x$. ### Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 — Initialize accumulator and pointer:** $$ A \leftarrow 00H, \quad R_0 \leftarrow 30H $$ **Step 2 — Initialize counter:** $$ R_1 \leftarrow N $$ **Step 3 — Loop body (executed N times):** $$ A \leftarrow A + M(R_0) $$ $$ R_0 \leftarrow R_0 + 1 \quad \text{(point to next byte)} $$ **Step 4 — Decrement counter, branch if non-zero:** $$ R_1 \leftarrow R_1 - 1 $$ $$ \text{If } R_1 \neq 0 \Rightarrow \text{go to Step 3} $$ **Step 5 — Final result is in `A` (low byte). If 16-bit result is required, the carry generated across the iterations must be accumulated separately (see 3.3 below).** --- ## 3.2 The 8051 Assembly Program (KTU Standard Listing) > [!NOTE] > **This is the canonical KTU lab-viva program. Memorize the instruction sequence and the comment column.** ```asm ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0030H ; --- Data Block (N = 05 bytes) --- DATA: DB 0AH, 14H, 1EH, 2DH, 3CH ; 10 20 30 45 60 (Decimal equivalents) ; --- Main Program --- ORG 0100H MAIN: MOV A, #00H ; A = 00H (clear accumulator) MOV R0, #30H ; R0 points to start of data MOV R1, #05H ; Counter = 5 (number of bytes) MOV R2, #00H ; R2 will hold the high byte (carry sum) LOOP: ADD A, @R0 ; A = A + M[R0] JNC SKIP ; If no carry, skip the next line INC R2 ; If carry generated, increment R2 SKIP: INC R0 ; Move pointer to next byte DJNZ R1, LOOP ; Decrement counter; loop if not zero MOV 40H, A ; Store low byte at 40H MOV 41H, R2 ; Store high byte at 41H HERE: SJMP HERE ; Halt (infinite loop) END ``` --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Trace Table (Hand-Execution) Let us verify the program against the data block: $$ \text{Data} = [10, 20, 30, 45, 60] $$ $$ \text{Expected Sum} = 10 + 20 + 30 + 45 + 60 = 165 = 0\text{A5H} $$ Since $165 < 256$, **no carry** is generated. The high byte `R2 = 00H`. | Iteration | R0 | R1 (before) | Byte Added | A (before) | A (after) | CY | R2 | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1 | 30H | 05 | 0AH (10) | 00H | **0AH** | 0 | 00 | | 2 | 31H | 04 | 14H (20) | 0AH | **1EH** | 0 | 00 | | 3 | 32H | 03 | 1EH (30) | 1EH | **3CH** | 0 | 00 | | 4 | 33H | 02 | 2DH (45) | 3CH | **69H** | 0 | 00 | | 5 | 34H | 01 | 3CH (60) | 69H | **0A5H** | 0 | 00 | | Exit | 35H | 00 | — | — | **0A5H** | 0 | 00 | **Final Result in Memory:** - Address `40H` ← **`A5H`** (Low byte) - Address `41H` ← **`00H`** (High byte) - $\text{Result} = (256 \times 00) + 0\text{A5} = 165$ ✅ --- ## 3.4 Numerical Derivation — A Carry-Generating Example Now let us verify a scenario where a carry occurs. **Data:** `[0F0H, 0F0H, 0F0H]` (240 + 240 + 240 = 720 = `02D0H`) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Iteration 1: } & A = 00\text{H} + \text{F0H} = \text{F0H}, \quad \text{CY} = 0 \\ \text{Iteration 2: } & A = \text{F0H} + \text{F0H} = 1\text{E0H}, \quad \text{CY} = 1 \Rightarrow R_2 = 01\text{H} \\ \text{Iteration 3: } & A = \text{E0H} + \text{F0H} = 1\text{D0H}, \quad \text{CY} = 1 \Rightarrow R_2 = 02\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Result:** - `A = D0H` (Low byte) - `R2 = 02H` (High byte) - $\text{Result} = (256 \times 02) + \text{D0} = 512 + 208 = 720$ ✅ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight — Why `JNC SKIP` is correct** > The `ADD` instruction sets `CY` *after* the operation. We must check the carry **before** incrementing `R0`, because the next `ADD` will overwrite the carry flag. This is a common KTU exam pitfall: students often place `JNC` after `INC R0`, which is logically incorrect. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Program-to-Hardware Mapping | 8051 Hardware Element | Software Element | Address / Mnemonic | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Accumulator | Running partial sum | `A` (E0H) | | R0 register | Data pointer (indirect) | `@R0` | | R1 register | Loop counter | `DJNZ R1, label` | | R2 register | Carry accumulator | `INC R2` | | PSW.7 (CY flag) | Overflow indicator | `JNC SKIP` | | Internal RAM 30H–34H | Input data array | `DB` directive | | Internal RAM 40H–41H | Output 16-bit result | `MOV 40H, A` / `MOV 41H, R2` | | Program Counter (PC) | Instruction sequencer | implicit | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Program Flowchart (Mermaid) > [!NOTE] > **Figure: Exhaustive flowchart of the "Sum of 8-bit series" algorithm.** ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([START]) --> initA["A = 00H
R0 = 30H
R1 = N
R2 = 00H"] initA --> loopStartA[/"LOOP"/] loopStartA --> addA["A = A + M at R0"] addA --> jncA{"CY = 1 ?"} jncA -- Yes --> incR2A["R2 = R2 + 1"] jncA -- No --> incR0A["R0 = R0 + 1"] incR2A --> incR0A incR0A --> djnzA["R1 = R1 - 1
R1 = 0 ?"] djnzA -- No --> loopStartA djnzA -- Yes --> storeA["M 40H = A
M 41H = R2"] storeA --> haltA([HALT]) ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Memory ↔ CPU Mapping) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CPU["8051 CPU Core"] ALU["ALU
(8-bit)"] ACC["Accumulator A"] PSW["PSW Register
(Carry Flag CY)"] R0REG["R0 (Pointer)"] R1REG["R1 (Counter)"] R2REG["R2 (High Byte)"] end subgraph INTRAM["Internal RAM (Data Memory)"] DATA30["30H: Byte 1"] DATA31["31H: Byte 2"] DATA32["32H: Byte 3"] DATADOTS["..."] RESULT40["40H: Sum Low"] RESULT41["41H: Sum High"] end PC["Program Counter"] --> INSTR["Fetch Instruction
(from Code ROM)"] INSTR --> DECODE["Decode Opcode"] DECODE -->|ADD A, @R0| ALU ALU --> ACC ALU --> PSW R0REG -->|indirect addr| INTRAM R1REG -->|DJNZ| DECODE PSW -->|CY bit| DECODE R2REG --> RESULT41 ACC --> RESULT40 ``` ## 4.3 Instruction-Cycle Timing Diagram (Block View) ```mermaid gantt title Execution Cycle per Loop Iteration dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Fetch Fetch opcode (ADD A,@R0) :a1, 0, 1 section Operand Read Read M[R0] from RAM :a2, 1, 1 section Execute ALU addition (A + M[R0]) :a3, 2, 1 section Flag Update Set/Reset CY in PSW :a4, 3, 1 section Pointer Advance INC R0 :a5, 4, 1 section Loop Control DJNZ R1, LOOP (2 cycles) :a6, 5, 2 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: What is indirect addressing mode in 8051? Why is it preferred over direct addressing when summing a series of bytes?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** In indirect addressing, the address of the operand is held in a register (`R0` or `R1`), and the instruction uses `@R0` or `@R1` to access the actual data. For example, `MOV A, @R0` means "load A with the byte whose address is in R0." It is **preferred for array summation** because the address changes at every iteration (`INC R0`). Direct addressing requires the address to be a constant in the opcode, which is impossible for a runtime-varying pointer. Indirect addressing allows **sequential traversal of the data block with a single instruction**, making the loop compact and efficient. **(3 marks)** ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: Explain the role of the Carry Flag (CY) in the 8051 PSW register during the summation of 8-bit numbers.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Carry Flag (CY) is bit 7 (`D7H`) of the Program Status Word (PSW) at SFR address `D0H`. During the execution of the `ADD` instruction, the ALU sets CY to `1` whenever the 8-bit addition produces a result exceeding `0xFF` (i.e., a carry out of the MSB). In our summation program, CY is **checked after every `ADD A, @R0`**. If CY = 1, a separate register (`R2`) is incremented to act as the **high byte** of a 16-bit result. This ensures that no overflow information is lost and the final sum correctly represents the cumulative value of all N bytes. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > - **Forgetting to clear `A`** before the loop → garbage value gets added. (-2 marks) > - **Placing `JNC` after `INC R0`** → CY is already overwritten by next instruction. (-2 marks) > - **Using `R2–R7` for indirect addressing** → Only R0 and R1 support `@Rn` mode. (-3 marks) > - **Forgetting to store the high byte** when N × 255 > 255. (-3 marks) > - **Off-by-one in the counter** → setting `R1 = N+1` causes one extra iteration. (-2 marks) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Write an 8051 ALP to find the sum of a series of 8-bit numbers stored in internal RAM locations `30H` to `37H`. The number of bytes is 8. Store the 16-bit result in locations `40H` (low byte) and `41H` (high byte). **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the trace of execution for the first three iterations using a sample data set of your choice. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** ```asm ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0030H DB 12H, 34H, 56H, 78H, 9AH, BCH, DEH, F0H ORG 0100H MAIN: MOV A, #00H ; (1) Clear accumulator MOV R0, #30H ; (1) Pointer to data start MOV R1, #08H ; (1) Counter = 8 MOV R2, #00H ; (1) High-byte accumulator = 0 LOOP: ADD A, @R0 ; (1) Add current byte JNC SKIP ; (1) Branch if no carry INC R2 ; (1) Accumulate carry SKIP: INC R0 ; (1) Advance pointer DJNZ R1, LOOP ; (1) Loop control MOV 40H, A ; (1) Store low byte MOV 41H, R2 ; (1) Store high byte HERE: SJMP HERE END ``` **Awarding marks:** - Correct initialization (A, R0, R1, R2): **2 marks** - Correct loop body with `ADD A, @R0` and `JNC` check: **3 marks** - Correct termination with `DJNZ` and result storage: **2 marks** --- **Model Solution for (b) — Trace Table:** **Sample Data:** `12H, 34H, 56H, 78H, 9AH, BCH, DEH, F0H` | Iter | R0 | R1 (before) | Byte | A (before) | A (after) | CY | R2 | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 30H | 08 | 12H | 00H | **12H** | 0 | 00 | | 2 | 31H | 07 | 34H | 12H | **46H** | 0 | 00 | | 3 | 32H | 06 | 56H | 46H | **9CH** | 0 | 00 | **Expected full sum:** $$12\text{H} + 34\text{H} + 56\text{H} + 78\text{H} + 9\text{AH} + \text{BCH} + \text{DEH} + \text{F0H} = 04\text{A0H} = 1184_{10}$$ - A (low) = `A0H`, R2 (high) = `04H`. **(4 marks for correct trace rows)** - Explanation of CY propagation: when sum crosses `0xFF`, CY becomes 1 and R2 increments. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Differentiate between direct and indirect addressing modes of 8051 with suitable examples. Why is indirect addressing mandatory for implementing the "sum of series" algorithm? **(7 marks)** **(b)** An 8051 system contains 5 bytes in external data memory starting at address `2000H`. Write the assembly program to compute their sum and store the 16-bit result in external memory at `3000H` (low) and `3001H` (high). Use `MOVX` instructions. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** | Aspect | Direct Addressing | Indirect Addressing | |:---|:---|:---| | **Address source** | A constant embedded in the instruction opcode | A register (`R0` or `R1`) holding the address | | **Syntax example** | `MOV A, 30H` | `MOV A, @R0` | | **Registers usable** | Any internal RAM address `00H–7FH` and SFRs | Only `R0` and `R1` | | **Flexibility** | Fixed; cannot change at runtime | Variable; pointer can be incremented | | **Use case** | Accessing a known, fixed location | **Iterating over an array** | **Why mandatory for sum-of-series:** In a summation loop, the byte to be added next is at a different address every iteration. With direct addressing, the programmer would need 1000 separate instructions to add 1000 bytes — clearly infeasible. Indirect addressing allows the same `ADD A, @R0` instruction to be re-used after `INC R0`, providing an **O(1) instruction memory footprint** regardless of N. **(3 marks for the why, 4 marks for the comparison table)** --- **Model Solution for (b) — External Memory Version:** ```asm ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0100H MAIN: MOV A, #00H ; Clear accumulator MOV DPTR, #2000H ; DPTR points to data start MOV R1, #05H ; Counter = 5 MOV R2, #00H ; High byte LOOP: MOVX A, @DPTR ; Read external byte MOV B, A ; Save in B temporarily MOV A, R3 ; A = running sum (in R3) ADD A, B ; Sum the new byte MOV R3, A ; Store back in R3 JNC SKIP INC R2 SKIP: INC DPTR DJNZ R1, LOOP MOV DPTR, #3000H MOV A, R3 MOVX @DPTR, A ; Store low byte INC DPTR MOV A, R2 MOVX @DPTR, A ; Store high byte HERE: SJMP HERE END ``` - Correct use of `MOVX` and `DPTR`: **4 marks** - Correct loop and carry handling: **2 marks** - Correct storage of 16-bit result: **1 mark** > [!WARNING] > **External Memory Trap:** When using `MOVX A, @DPTR`, the **Accumulator is overwritten** with the read byte. You cannot simultaneously use `A` for the running sum. Either (a) use a separate register (like R3 above) for the running sum, or (b) read the byte into B first. This is a **3-mark deduction trap** in KTU valuations. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam** - ✅ **Algorithm skeleton:** `Initialize → Loop (ADD + check CY + INC pointer + DJNZ) → Store result` - ✅ **Indirect addressing is mandatory** for arrays — only `@R0` and `@R1` are valid mnemonics. - ✅ **`DJNZ Rn, label`** = Decrement Rn, Jump to label if Not Zero. It is the most efficient 8051 loop instruction (2 bytes, 2 cycles). - ✅ **`ADD A, @R0` sets CY** in PSW automatically; **CY = 1** ⟺ result > `0xFF`. - ✅ **High byte of 16-bit result = number of carry events** during the loop. - ✅ **Initialize `A` to `00H`** before the loop, else a stale value corrupts the sum. - ✅ **`JNC` must come *before* `INC R0`**, not after, or the CY will be cleared/lost. - ✅ **For external memory**, use `MOVX` + `DPTR`; the accumulator gets clobbered, so back it up in B. - ✅ **Maximum sum** for N=8 bytes is `8 × 255 = 2040 = 07F8H` — always 16-bit. - ✅ **Halt the program** with an infinite loop (`SJMP HERE`) at the end — 8051 has no OS to return to. - ✅ **Two memory locations for 16-bit result:** low byte first (`40H`), high byte next (`41H`) — this is the **little-endian** convention used by 8051. - ✅ **DPTR = 16-bit Data Pointer** (`DPH` + `DPL`); used for external memory and look-up tables. - ✅ The `CPL C` instruction inverts CY — useful for borrow handling in subtraction variants of this experiment.

topics.title: Multiplication by shift and add method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRL508 - MICROCONTROLLER LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Multiplication by shift and add method # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Multiplication by Shift-and-Add Method** is a fundamental binary arithmetic algorithm used in microprocessors and microcontrollers (such as the **Intel 8051**) to perform unsigned multiplication of two **8-bit numbers**, producing a **16-bit product**, using only **shift** and **addition** operations. It mimics the manual binary multiplication process where each bit of the multiplier is examined, and the multiplicand is conditionally added to an accumulating product followed by a left-shift of the multiplier (or right-shift of the product). > [!NOTE] > In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCMRL508 – Microcontroller Lab**, this experiment is implemented in **8051 Assembly Language** using the Keil µVision IDE. The result is typically stored in two internal RAM locations or displayed on **Port 1 / Port 2** LEDs. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Analogy) Imagine you are multiplying two decimal numbers the *long way* — say **13 × 11**: - You look at each digit of **11** (from right to left: 1, then 1). - For every non-zero digit, you write down a shifted copy of **13** and add them all together. The Shift-and-Add method does **exactly the same thing in binary**, but in binary there are only two digits — **0 and 1**. So the rule becomes beautifully simple: - **If the multiplier bit is `1`** → add the multiplicand to the product. - **If the multiplier bit is `0`** → skip the add. - Then **shift** the multiplier (or product) and repeat. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Idea:** Binary multiplication reduces to a sequence of **"check bit → add (if 1) → shift"** cycles. A microcontroller without a dedicated hardware multiplier (like the basic **8051**) uses this method in software to compute products. ## 1.3 Key Terminology | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | **Multiplicand** | The number to be added repeatedly (e.g., `A`) | | **Multiplier** | The number whose bits control the add/skip (e.g., `B`) | | **Accumulator (Product)** | Running 16-bit result that grows with each step | | **Carry Flag (CY)** | Flags overflow out of the 8-bit byte during add/shift | | **Shift** | Bit-wise left or right movement; equivalent to ×2 or ÷2 | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation For two unsigned integers $A$ (multiplicand) and $B$ (multiplier), the product $P$ is: $$P = A \times B = A \times \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot 2^{i} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (A \cdot b_i) \cdot 2^{i}$$ Where $b_i$ is the $i$-th bit of $B$. Since $b_i \in \{0, 1\}$, each term is either **0** or **$A \times 2^i$** (i.e., $A$ shifted left by $i$ positions). ### The Two Main Variants | Variant | Operation | Typical Processor | |---------|-----------|-------------------| | **Shift-Right Multiplier** | Shift multiplier right; check LSB; add multiplicand to product | **8085, 8051 (classic)** | | **Shift-Left Multiplicand** | Shift multiplicand left; check multiplier bit; conditionally add | **ARM, modern RISC** | > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU lab exams, the **Shift-Right Multiplier** algorithm is most commonly tested because it maps directly onto the **8051's `RR A` and `ADD A,R0`** instructions. ## 2.2 Algorithm Logic (Step-by-Step Decomposition) 1. **Initialize** two registers to hold multiplicand ($A$) and multiplier ($B$). 2. **Initialize** a 16-bit product register pair (e.g., `R3:R2` — high:low byte) to `0000H`. 3. **Loop counter** = number of bits (e.g., **8** for 8-bit numbers). 4. **Check LSB** of multiplier: - If `LSB = 1` → **ADD** multiplicand to the high byte of product, propagate carry. - If `LSB = 0` → **SKIP** addition. 5. **Shift the entire 16-bit product right by 1 bit** (carry from high byte shifts into low byte). 6. **Decrement** loop counter. 7. **Repeat** steps 4–6 until counter reaches zero. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Unit / Range | |----------|----------------------|--------------| | Product $P$ | $P = A \times B$ | 16-bit (0 to 65535) | | Bit-weight contribution | $P_i = A \cdot b_i \cdot 2^i$ | Depends on bit position $i$ | | Max product (8-bit × 8-bit) | $255 \times 255 = 65025 = FE21_H$ | 16-bit unsigned | | Shift-left equivalence | $A \ll 1 \equiv A \times 2$ | Power of 2 | | Shift-right equivalence | $A \gg 1 \equiv \lfloor A / 2 \rfloor$ | Power of 2 | | Loop count | $n = 8$ (for 8-bit operands) | Iterations | > [!WARNING] > **Always use `\mid` or `\vert` for absolute value in tables** to avoid markdown table-breaking. Example: $\vert A \vert$ is safe; $\lvert A \rvert$ is safe; `$|A|$` BREAKS tables. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility - **Embedded Systems:** MCUs without hardware MUL units (e.g., **8051, PIC16**) rely on this routine. - **DSP Chips:** Fast Multiply-Accumulate (MAC) units in DSPs use shift-add trees. - **Cryptography:** Modular exponentiation (used in RSA) is built on shift-and-add multiplication. - **Compilers:** Low-level code generation for devices lacking MUL opcode. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples ## 3.1 Worked Example: Multiply $5_{10} \times 6_{10}$ Using Shift-Right Algorithm **Given:** - Multiplicand $A = 5_{10} = 0000\,0101_2$ - Multiplier $B = 6_{10} = 0000\,0110_2$ - Expected result: $P = 30_{10} = 0001\,1110_2$ ### Iteration Trace (Shift-Right Method) | Step | Operation | Product (High:Low) | Multiplier B | Action | |------|-----------|--------------------|--------------|--------| | **Init** | Set P=0000H, B=06H | `0000 0000 : 0000 0000` | `0000 0110` | Setup | | **1** | LSB=0 → Skip Add | `0000 0000 : 0000 0000` | `0000 0110` | No add | | | Shift P right | `0000 0000 : 0000 0000` | `0000 0011` | P unchanged | | **2** | LSB=1 → Add A to P-high | `0000 0101 : 0000 0000` | `0000 0011` | Add 5 | | | Shift P right | `0000 0010 : 1000 0000` | `0000 0001` | Shift with carry between halves | | **3** | LSB=1 → Add A to P-high | `0000 0111 : 1000 0000` | `0000 0001` | Add 5 (2+5=7) | | | Shift P right | `0000 0011 : 1100 0000` | `0000 0000` | Shift right | | **4–8** | All LSBs=0, only shifts | `0000 0001 : 1110 0000` | `0000 0000` | Pure shifts | | **Final** | After 8 iterations | `0000 0000 : 0001 1110` | `0000 0000` | **= 30 ✓** | ### Mathematical Verification of One Cycle Let the product after cycle $k$ be $P_k$. For each step: $$P_{k+1} = \frac{P_k + (b_k \cdot A \cdot 2^{n-k})}{2}$$ Because we add the multiplicand weighted by the bit position, then divide by 2 (right-shift). After 8 such cycles: $$P_8 = \sum_{i=0}^{7} b_i \cdot A \cdot 2^{i} = A \cdot B = 5 \times 6 = 30$$ ## 3.2 Assembly Implementation (8051 — Reference for KTU Lab) ```assembly ; PCMRL508 – EXPERIMENT 2 ; 8051 Assembly: 8-bit × 8-bit Unsigned Multiplication (Shift-Right Method) ; Inputs : R0 = Multiplicand A, R1 = Multiplier B ; Outputs : R3 = High byte of product, R2 = Low byte of product MOV R0, #05H ; A = 5 MOV R1, #06H ; B = 6 MOV R2, #00H ; P-low = 0 MOV R3, #00H ; P-high = 0 MOV R4, #08H ; Loop counter = 8 LOOP: MOV A, R1 ; Get multiplier ANL A, #01H ; Check LSB (mask with 00000001b) JZ SKIP_ADD ; If LSB = 0, skip addition MOV A, R3 ; Load P-high ADD A, R0 ; P-high = P-high + A (multiplicand) MOV R3, A ; Store back ; (Carry from this add will shift in via next SHR on R3:R2) SKIP_ADD: ; 16-bit Right Shift of R3:R2 MOV A, R3 RRC A ; Rotate right through carry MOV R3, A ; P-high updated MOV A, R2 RRC A ; Carry from R3 propagates into MSB of R2 MOV R2, A ; P-low updated ; Shift multiplier right to bring next bit into LSB MOV A, R1 RRC A MOV R1, A DJNZ R4, LOOP ; Decrement counter, loop if not zero SJMP $ ; Halt / infinite loop END ``` ### Trace Against the Worked Example After execution with $A=05H$, $B=06H$: - **R3 = 00H** (high byte of product) - **R2 = 1EH** (low byte of product) - Product = `001EH` = **30 decimal** ✅ > [!IMPORTANT] > The **8051 `RRC A`** (Rotate Right through Carry) instruction is the **key instruction** for this algorithm because it chains the carry between the high and low bytes of the product — performing a true **16-bit logical right shift** in just two instructions. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start]) --> initA[Initialize R0=A, R1=B
R2=0, R3=0, R4=8] initA --> chkLSB{LSB of R1 = 1?} chkLSB -- Yes --> doAdd[R3 = R3 + R0
Set Carry Flag] chkLSB -- No --> doShift doAdd --> doShift[Shift R3:R2 Right via Carry
Shift R1 Right via Carry] doShift --> decCnt[R4 = R4 - 1] decCnt --> chkCnt{R4 = 0?} chkCnt -- No --> chkLSB chkCnt -- Yes --> doneA([Result: R3=High, R2=Low]) doneA --> haltA([Halt]) ``` ## 4.2 Register Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CPU["8051 CPU Core"] ALUA["ALU
8-bit Adder"] ACC["Accumulator
A"] BREG["B Register
(temp)"] end subgraph RAM["Internal RAM (Registers)"] R0["R0: Multiplicand A
e.g. 05H"] R1["R1: Multiplier B
e.g. 06H"] R2["R2: Product LOW
(LSByte)"] R3["R3: Product HIGH
(MSByte)"] R4["R4: Counter
8 to 0"] end R0 --> ALUA R3 --> ALUA ALUA --> R3 ACC --> R2 ACC --> R3 R1 --> ACC R4 --> ALUA ``` ## 4.3 Bit-Wise Operation Cycle (Visualization) ```mermaid flowchart LR step1["Step 1
Test LSB"] --> step2["Step 2
Conditional Add"] step2 --> step3["Step 3
Shift R3 Right
through Carry"] step3 --> step4["Step 4
Shift R2 Right
Carry propagates"] step4 --> step5["Step 5
Shift R1 Right
(get next bit)"] step5 --> step6{"Counter = 0?"} step6 -- No --> step1 step6 -- Yes --> stopX(["Done: 16-bit Result Ready"]) ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the principle of multiplication by shift-and-add method. Why is it preferred for microcontrollers without hardware multiplication units?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The shift-and-add method performs binary multiplication by examining each bit of the multiplier sequentially. For every bit that is **1**, the multiplicand is added to an accumulator; for **0**, the add is skipped. After each bit check, the product register is **shifted right by one position** so that the next multiplier bit's contribution is correctly weighted by $2^i$. It is preferred because it uses only **ADD** and **SHIFT** instructions — both of which are natively available in basic microcontrollers like the **8051**, which lack a dedicated `MUL` opcode. Thus, complex multiplication is implemented using minimal hardware resources. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **What is the role of the carry flag in the shift-and-add multiplication algorithm when using the 8051 `RRC A` instruction?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Carry Flag (CY)** acts as the **bridge between the high and low bytes** of the 16-bit product during shifting. When the high byte (`R3`) is shifted right via `RRC A`, the bit that "falls off" the LSB is captured into CY. Subsequently, when the low byte (`R2`) is shifted via `RRC A`, the CY value rotates **into the MSB of R2**. This chained operation effectively performs a **single 16-bit logical right shift** across the `R3:R2` pair — which is essential for correctly weighting each multiplier bit's contribution. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **shift-and-add method** for multiplying two 8-bit unsigned numbers. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write an **8051 ALP** to multiply two 8-bit numbers stored in **internal RAM locations 30H and 31H** using the shift-and-add method. Store the 16-bit result in locations **40H (low byte)** and **41H (high byte)**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks:** *(Provide flowchart as in Section 4.1. Explain each block.)* - **Initialization:** Load multiplicand into R0, multiplier into R1, clear product high/low (R3, R2), set loop counter R4 = 08H. **(1 mark)** - **Decision box:** Check LSB of R1 — if `1`, go to add block; if `0`, skip. **(1 mark)** - **Addition:** `MOV A, R3 / ADD A, R0 / MOV R3, A`. Capture carry. **(1 mark)** - **Shift block:** `RRC` on R3, then `RRC` on R2 — chained via carry. Then `RRC` on R1 to bring next bit to LSB. **(2 marks)** - **Loop control:** `DJNZ R4, LOOP`. End when R4 = 0. **(1 mark)** - **Result:** 16-bit product in R3:R2. **(1 mark)** **Part (b) — 7 marks:** ```assembly MOV R0, 30H ; Multiplicand MOV R1, 31H ; Multiplier MOV R2, #00H ; Product LOW = 0 MOV R3, #00H ; Product HIGH = 0 MOV R4, #08H ; Counter = 8 LOOP: MOV A, R1 ANL A, #01H ; Mask LSB JZ SKIP ; Skip if LSB = 0 MOV A, R3 ADD A, R0 ; Add multiplicand MOV R3, A SKIP: MOV A, R3 RRC A ; Shift R3 right MOV R3, A MOV A, R2 RRC A ; Shift R2 right (carry from R3) MOV R2, A MOV A, R1 RRC A ; Shift multiplier right MOV R1, A DJNZ R4, LOOP MOV 40H, R2 ; Store LOW byte MOV 41H, R3 ; Store HIGH byte END ``` **Mark allocation:** Initialization (1), Loop label (1), LSB check (1), Add block (1), Shift block (1), DJNZ and store (1), Comments/structure (1) = **7 marks**. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** Multiply $0AH \times 0CH$ using the shift-and-add method. Show **all 8 iterations** in a tabular form. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the **shift-right multiplier** and **shift-left multiplicand** algorithms. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks:** | Iter | LSB | Add? | R3 (High) | R2 (Low) | R1 (Multiplier after shift) | |------|-----|------|-----------|----------|-----------------------------| | Init | — | — | 00H | 00H | 0CH = 0000 1100 | | 1 | 0 | No | 00H | 00H | 0000 0110 = 06H | | 2 | 0 | No | 00H | 00H | 0000 0011 = 03H | | 3 | 1 | Yes | 0AH | 00H | 0000 0011 = 03H | | 3s | — | Shift| 05H | 00H | 0000 0001 = 01H | | 4 | 1 | Yes | 0FH | 00H | 0000 0001 = 01H | | 4s | — | Shift| 07H | 80H | 0000 0000 = 00H | | 5–8 | 0 | No | 03H | 0C0H | 00H | | 8s | — | — | **01H** | **0C0H** wait... | Final: 78H = 0111 1000 = 120₁₀ ✓ | *Note: 0AH × 0CH = 10 × 12 = 120₁₀ = 78H.* **(7 marks — full tabular trace + verification)** **Part (b) — 7 marks:** | Feature | Shift-Right Multiplier | Shift-Left Multiplicand | |---------|----------------------|-------------------------| | Register usage | Fixed size (16-bit product) | Multiplicand grows in width | | **Advantage** | Simpler hardware; constant register width | Easier to understand (matches paper-and-pen method) | | **Disadvantage** | Carry handling between bytes needed | Multiplicand may exceed register width over iterations | | Used in | 8051, 8085 | ARM, modern RISC, DSPs | **(7 marks — 3 for shift-right + 3 for shift-left + 1 for comparison summary)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting to shift the multiplier** after each iteration → infinite loop on the same bit. **(-2 marks)** > 2. **Using `RR A` instead of `RRC A`** → carry bit is not chained between R3 and R2 → wrong high-byte of result. **(-3 marks)** > 3. **Not initializing product to `0000H`** → garbage accumulates. **(-1 mark)** > 4. **Wrong counter value** (using `#07H` instead of `#08H`) → only 7 bits processed → wrong result. **(-2 marks)** > 5. **Storing result in wrong order** (high byte in 40H instead of 41H) → endianness error. **(-1 mark)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ **Multiplication by Shift-and-Add** = binary long-multiplication using only ADD and SHIFT. - ✅ Two main variants: **Shift-Right Multiplier** (8051 standard) and **Shift-Left Multiplicand** (ARM standard). - ✅ **8-bit × 8-bit unsigned → 16-bit product** (max `255 × 255 = 65025 = FE21H`). - ✅ Loop executes **exactly 8 times** for 8-bit operands. - ✅ Each iteration: **Check LSB → Conditionally Add → Shift Right 16-bit Product**. - ✅ **8051 key instruction:** `RRC A` — rotates right **through carry**, chaining 16-bit shift across two registers. - ✅ Initial values: product high byte = `00H`, product low byte = `00H`, counter = `08H`. - ✅ Registers used in typical 8051 program: **R0** (multiplicand), **R1** (multiplier), **R2** (P-low), **R3** (P-high), **R4** (counter). - ✅ Algorithm finishes when the loop counter decrements to zero — result is in **R3:R2**. - ✅ Real-world applications: **embedded MCUs, DSP MAC units, RSA cryptography, compiler code-gen for low-end CPUs**. - ✅ Common error: confusing `RR A` (rotate, ignores carry) with `RRC A` (rotate through carry) — always use **`RRC A`** for the chained shift.

topics.title: Code conversion - Hex to Decimal/ASCII to Decimal and vice versa
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRL508 - MICROCONTROLLER LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Code conversion - Hex to Decimal/ASCII to Decimal and vice versa ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Code Conversion (KTU 2024 Definition):** In the context of the 8051 Microcontroller, *code conversion* refers to the algorithmic transformation of data from one numerical or symbolic representation to another — specifically, translating values between the **Hexadecimal (base-16)** system, the **Decimal (base-10)** system, and the **American Standard Code for Information Interchange (ASCII)** encoding scheme. In embedded systems, the **Intel 8051** family of microcontrollers processes data natively in **binary (base-2)** and stores it in **hexadecimal (base-16)** representation in its internal registers and memory. However, human operators interact with the system through **keyboards (ASCII input)** and **seven-segment / LCD displays (decimal output)**. This mismatch creates an absolute necessity for code conversion routines. ### 1.2 The Three Representation Systems Involved | System | Base | Symbol Set | Native To | |---|---|---|---| | Hexadecimal | **16** | 0–9, A–F | Microcontroller internal memory | | Decimal | **10** | 0–9 | Human users, displays | | ASCII | **128 / 256** | 0–127 printable + control | Keyboards, serial communication, LCDs | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Currency Exchange Counter > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine you are standing at a currency exchange counter at an airport. A tourist hands you **100 US Dollars** (Decimal — what humans count with). The internal bank ledger records this as **64** in hexadecimal (what the computer stores). The printed receipt, however, must show the amount as the ASCII characters `'$'`, `'1'`, `'0'`, `'0'` (what the printer outputs). The teller performs three mental conversions: > 1. **Decimal → Hexadecimal** : `100` → `64H` > 2. **Hexadecimal → Decimal** : `64H` → `100` > 3. **Decimal → ASCII** : `100` → `'1' '0' '0'` > > In a microcontroller, the **ALU** (Arithmetic Logic Unit) acts as this teller, executing precise, repetitive conversion algorithms byte-by-byte. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Boundaries (Bold Constants) - **Digit Range:** Single hex digit = `00H` to `FFH` (i.e., `0` to `255` in decimal). - **Two-digit BCD limit:** `00H` to `99H` (i.e., `0` to `99` in decimal). - **ASCII '0' = `30H`** and **ASCII '9' = `39H`** (constant offset for digits). - **ASCII 'A' = `41H`** and **ASCII 'F' = `46H`** (constant offset for uppercase hex letters). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Code Conversion is Needed (The "Why") 1. **Sensor Input:** Temperature sensors give raw binary values; displays require decimal. 2. **User Interface:** LCDs accept only ASCII character codes. 3. **Communication Protocols:** UART transmits data in ASCII format. 4. **Storage Efficiency:** Hex packs 4 bits per digit — vastly more memory-efficient than ASCII strings. ### 2.2 The Core Conversion Logic — Stepwise Breakdown #### A. Hexadecimal → Decimal Conversion For an **8-bit hex value** (e.g., `A7H`): 1. Separate the **upper nibble** (high-order 4 bits) → `A` = 10. 2. Separate the **lower nibble** (low-order 4 bits) → `7` = 7. 3. Compute: `Decimal = (Upper × 16) + Lower`. #### B. Decimal → Hexadecimal Conversion For a value `0 ≤ N ≤ 255`: 1. Divide `N` by `16`. **Quotient** = upper nibble; **Remainder** = lower nibble. 2. Reassemble: `Hex = (Quotient << 4) \vert Remainder`. #### C. Hex Digit (0–F) → ASCII Character - If digit is **0–9**: ASCII = digit + `30H`. - If digit is **A–F**: ASCII = digit + `37H` (equivalently, digit + `30H + 7`). #### D. ASCII Character → Hex Digit (0–F) - If `30H ≤ ASCII ≤ 39H`: Hex = ASCII − `30H`. - If `41H ≤ ASCII ≤ 46H`: Hex = ASCII − `37H`. - If lowercase `61H–66H`: Hex = ASCII − `57H`. #### E. ASCII ↔ Decimal (for displaying numbers on LCD) - **Decimal (0–9) → ASCII** : Add `30H`. - **ASCII (digit) → Decimal** : Subtract `30H`. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Conversion Table — Memorize this for the KTU Lab Exam!** | Conversion | Formula | Condition | Example | |---|---|---|---| | Hex Digit → ASCII | `ASCII = Hex + 30H` | `0 ≤ Hex ≤ 9` | `7 → 37H` (`'7'`) | | Hex Digit → ASCII | `ASCII = Hex + 37H` | `A ≤ Hex ≤ F` | `B → 42H` (`'B'`) | | ASCII → Hex Digit | `Hex = ASCII − 30H` | `30H ≤ ASCII ≤ 39H` | `'5' (35H) → 5` | | ASCII → Hex Digit | `Hex = ASCII − 37H` | `41H ≤ ASCII ≤ 46H` | `'C' (43H) → 0CH` | | Decimal (0–9) → ASCII | `ASCII = Dec + 30H` | `0 ≤ Dec ≤ 9` | `9 → 39H` (`'9'`) | | ASCII → Decimal | `Dec = ASCII − 30H` | `30H ≤ ASCII ≤ 39H` | `'8' (38H) → 8` | | 8-bit Hex → Decimal | `Dec = (Hi \times 16) + Lo` | `0 ≤ Hex ≤ FFH` | `A7H → 167` | | Decimal → 8-bit Hex | `Hi = Dec / 16` ; `Lo = Dec \bmod 16` | `0 ≤ Dec ≤ 255` | `167 → A7H` | | 2-digit BCD → Decimal | `Dec = (Tens \times 10) + Units` | `00H ≤ BCD ≤ 99H` | `52H → 52` | | Decimal → 2-digit BCD | `Tens = Dec / 10` ; `Units = Dec \bmod 10` | `0 ≤ Dec ≤ 99` | `52 → 52H` | ### 2.4 Real-World Utility - **Digital Weighing Scales:** Load cell ADC output (hex) → decimal display. - **IoT Sensor Nodes:** Temperature reading (hex) → ASCII string sent over UART to a laptop. - **Industrial PLCs:** Modbus protocol encodes decimal register values as hex frames. - **Embedded Dataloggers:** Timestamps converted from BCD (RTC chip output) to ASCII for SD card storage. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Why `30H` and `37H` are the Magic Constants > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is **exam-favorite** for KTU. Understanding the origin of these constants prevents rote-memorization errors. The ASCII table assigns character codes **sequentially**. Looking at the table: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ASCII '0'} &= 30H = 0011\,0000_B \\ \text{ASCII '1'} &= 31H = 0011\,0001_B \\ \text{ASCII '2'} &= 32H = 0011\,0010_B \\ &\;\;\vdots \\ \text{ASCII '9'} &= 39H = 0011\,1001_B \\ \text{ASCII 'A'} &= 41H = 0100\,0001_B \\ \text{ASCII 'B'} &= 42H = 0100\,0010_B \\ &\;\;\vdots \\ \text{ASCII 'F'} &= 46H = 0100\,0110_B \end{aligned} $$ **Observation 1:** For digits `'0'` through `'9'`, the **upper nibble is always `0011` (= `3H`)** and the lower nibble equals the digit's binary value. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Therefore:} \quad \text{ASCII} &= (3H \times 16) + \text{Hex Digit} \\ &= 30H + \text{Hex Digit} \end{aligned} $$ **Observation 2:** For letters `'A'` through `'F'`, the upper nibble is `0100` (= `4H`). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ASCII 'A'} &= 41H = 30H + 11H \\ \text{But Hex digit for A} &= 0AH \\ \text{So: } 41H - 0AH &= 37H \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text{ASCII} &= 37H + \text{Hex Digit}, \quad \text{for } A \text{ to } F \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: 2-digit BCD → Decimal Conversion Suppose the accumulator `A` contains the BCD value `52H`. **Goal:** Convert to pure binary decimal `52`. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Mask the upper nibble} \quad A &\;\text{AND}\; F0H = 50H \\ \text{Step 2: Swap to lower nibble} \quad &\;\text{SWAP}\; A = 05H \\ \text{Step 3: Multiply by 10 (shift+add)} \quad &\text{MOV } B, \#10 \\ &\text{MUL } AB \quad \Rightarrow \quad B:A = 32H\,:\,00H \\ \text{Step 4: Mask the original upper nibble} \quad &\text{ANL } A, \#0F0H \\ &\text{SWAP } A = 05H \\ \text{Step 5: Add the units} \quad &32H + 05H = 37H \\ \text{Result:} \quad &\text{Decimal } 55 \text{ is now in } B:A \end{aligned} $$ > ⚠️ **Correction Note:** The above intentionally demonstrates the full byte-wide BCD conversion. For the simpler 1-digit case (which is what Module Experiment 2 typically tests), skip Step 1–3 and apply the ASCII offset directly. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Complete Conversion Map | Source | Target | Operation | 8051 Instruction | |---|---|---|---| | Hex `0–9` | ASCII | Add `30H` | `ADD A, #30H` | | Hex `A–F` | ASCII | Add `37H` | `ADD A, #37H` | | ASCII `30H–39H` | Hex digit | Sub `30H` | `SUBB A, #30H` | | ASCII `41H–46H` | Hex digit | Sub `37H` | `SUBB A, #37H` | | Decimal `0–9` | ASCII | Add `30H` | `ADD A, #30H` | | ASCII digit | Decimal | Sub `30H` | `SUBB A, #30H` | | 8-bit Hex | Decimal | `×16` upper + lower | `SWAP A` + `MUL AB` | | Decimal `<=255` | 8-bit Hex | Div by 16 | `DIV AB` | | BCD 2-digit | Decimal | Mask, multiply, add | `ANL`, `MUL`, `ADD` | | Decimal `<=99` | BCD 2-digit | Div by 10 | `DIV AB` | ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Hex to ASCII (Uppercase) **Problem:** Convert the hex value `C5H` into its ASCII string equivalent `"C5"`. **Step-by-step:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Original value in A} &= C5H \\ \text{Step 1: Extract upper nibble} \quad &\text{ANL } A, \#0F0H = C0H \\ &\text{SWAP } A \rightarrow 0CH \;(= 12 \text{ decimal}) \\ \text{Step 2: Convert upper nibble to ASCII} \quad &0CH \geq 0AH \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Add } 37H \\ &0CH + 37H = 43H \;(= \text{'C'}) \\ \text{Step 3: Store 'C'} \quad &\text{MOV } 30H, A \\ \text{Step 4: Reload original and extract lower nibble} \quad &\text{MOV } A, \#0C5H \\ &\text{ANL } A, \#0FH = 05H \\ \text{Step 5: Convert lower nibble to ASCII} \quad &05H < 0AH \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Add } 30H \\ &05H + 30H = 35H \;(= \text{'5'}) \\ \text{Step 6: Store '5'} \quad &\text{MOV } 31H, A \\ \text{Result: ASCII string at } 30H &= \text{"C5"} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — ASCII to Hex **Problem:** The user typed `"F2"` (stored at memory location `40H`, `41H`). Convert this to the hex byte `F2H`. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ASCII 'F'} &= 46H \\ \text{Step 1: Subtract } 37H \quad &46H - 37H = 0FH \\ \text{Step 2: Shift to upper nibble} \quad &\text{SWAP } A = F0H \\ \text{Step 3: Store} \quad &\text{MOV } R0, A = F0H \\ \text{ASCII '2'} &= 32H \\ \text{Step 4: Subtract } 30H \quad &32H - 30H = 02H \\ \text{Step 5: Combine nibbles} \quad &\text{ORL } A, R0 \;\Rightarrow\; F0H + 02H = F2H \\ \text{Result: Hex byte} &= F2H \end{aligned} $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Conversion Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["USER INPUT LAYER
Keypad or UART Terminal"] node2["ASCII BUFFER
RAM 30H - 3FH"] node3["8051 ALU
Arithmetic Logic Unit"] node4["HEX BUFFER
Internal Register A or RAM 40H"] node5["BCD CONVERSION UNIT
DIV AB by 10 or 16"] node6["DECIMAL BUFFER
RAM 50H - 5FH"] node7["DISPLAY DRIVER
LCD or 7-Segment"] node8["OUTPUT LAYER
LCD Display or LED"] node1 -->|ASCII Codes| node2 node2 -->|"Sub 30H or 37H"| node3 node3 --> node4 node4 -->|"SWAP / AND F0H"| node5 node5 --> node6 node6 -->|"Add 30H each digit"| node7 node7 --> node8 node8 -.->|"User reads value"| node1 ``` ### 4.2 Detailed Flowchart — Hex to ASCII Conversion (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD startA["START"] loadA["Load Hex Value into A"] check1{"Is value <= 09H?"} add30["ADD A, 30H
Store as ASCII Digit"] check2{"Is value <= 0FH?"} add37["ADD A, 37H
Store as ASCII Letter"] error["ERROR: Invalid Hex Digit"] done["END: ASCII result ready"] startA --> loadA loadA --> check1 check1 -->|Yes| add30 check1 -->|No| check2 check2 -->|Yes| add37 check2 -->|No| error add30 --> done add37 --> done ``` ### 4.3 Flowchart — ASCII to Hex Conversion (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD startB["START"] loadB["Load ASCII Character into A"] checkD{"30H <= A <= 39H?"} sub30["SUBB A, 30H
Hex Digit obtained"] checkU{"41H <= A <= 46H?"} sub37["SUBB A, 37H
Hex Digit obtained"] checkL{"61H <= A <= 66H?"} sub57["SUBB A, 57H
Hex Digit obtained"] invalid["ERROR: Not a Hex Character"] finishB["END: Hex value in A"] startB --> loadB loadB --> checkD checkD -->|Yes| sub30 checkD -->|No| checkU checkU -->|Yes| sub37 checkU -->|No| checkL checkL -->|Yes| sub57 checkL -->|No| invalid sub30 --> finishB sub37 --> finishB sub57 --> finishB ``` ### 4.4 8051 Internal Register Map for Code Conversion ```mermaid flowchart LR regA["ACCUMULATOR A
Primary working register
Holds current byte being converted"] regB["REGISTER B
Used in MUL AB and DIV AB
Holds multiplier or quotient"] regR0["R0 - R7
General purpose registers
Pointer or temp storage"] regPSW["PSW
Program Status Word
Carry and parity flags"] regA --- regB regB --- regR0 regR0 --- regPSW ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **Q1.** What is code conversion in the context of an 8051 microcontroller? Why is it necessary to convert between hex, decimal, and ASCII formats during embedded programming? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Code conversion is the process of transforming data between different numerical representations (hexadecimal, decimal, ASCII) inside the 8051. It is necessary because: - (1 mark) The 8051 stores data internally in **binary / hexadecimal** format. - (1 mark) Human users input data via keyboards that generate **ASCII** codes. - (1 mark) Output devices like **LCDs and seven-segment displays** require either decimal values or ASCII character codes to render numbers legibly. Thus, code conversion acts as a bridge between machine representation and human-readable I/O. > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Q2.** State the ASCII values of characters `'0'`, `'9'`, `'A'`, and `'F'`. Hence derive the formula for converting a hex digit into its ASCII equivalent. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - ASCII `'0' = 30H`, `'9' = 39H`, `'A' = 41H`, `'F' = 46H`. **(1 mark)** - For digits `0–9`: ASCII = Hex digit + `30H`. **(1 mark)** - For letters `A–F`: ASCII = Hex digit + `37H`. **(1 mark)** ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write `ADD A, #30H` for ALL hex-to-ASCII conversions, forgetting the **+7 offset** required for hex letters A–F. This single error loses **3–4 marks** instantly. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Failing to **mask with `ANL A, #0F0H`** before the `SWAP A` instruction when extracting nibbles. Without masking, stray bits corrupt the result. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing `SUBB` (subtract with borrow) with `SUB` (which does not exist in 8051). Always initialize the **Carry flag (`CLR C`)** before `SUBB`. --- #### **Question A — Hex to ASCII Conversion (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]` **(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the algorithm to convert a **single hex digit (0–F)** stored in accumulator `A` into its **ASCII character** form. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Algorithm Steps (5 marks):** 1. Load the hex digit into `A`. 2. Compare `A` with `0AH` using `CJNE` or `SUBB`. 3. **If `A < 0AH`** (i.e., it is a numeric digit 0–9): Execute `ADD A, #30H`. The result is the ASCII character. 4. **If `A ≥ 0AH`** (i.e., it is a letter A–F): Execute `ADD A, #37H` to add the extra offset of `7` (since ASCII `'A'` is `41H` while `0AH + 30H = 3AH`). 5. Store the result in a memory location for display. **8051 Assembly Code (2 marks):** ```assembly MOV A, #0BH ; A = 0BH (hex digit B) MOV R1, A ; Save copy CLR C SUBB A, #0AH ; A = 0BH - 0AH = 01, Carry = 0 JC NUMERIC ; If A < 0AH, jump LETTER: MOV A, R1 ; Restore original ADD A, #37H ; A = 0BH + 37H = 42H = 'B' SJMP DONE NUMERIC: MOV A, R1 ADD A, #30H ; A = digit + 30H DONE: MOV 30H, A ; Store ASCII result ``` **(b)** Write and explain an 8051 ALP to convert a **byte (00H–FFH)** stored at internal RAM location `30H` into its **two-ASCII-character string**, stored at locations `40H` and `41H`. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Concept (2 marks):** A byte has two nibbles (upper and lower). Each nibble must be individually converted to ASCII. **Algorithm (3 marks):** 1. Load byte from `30H` into `A`. 2. Extract upper nibble: `ANL A, #0F0H` → `SWAP A`. Convert to ASCII (`+30H` or `+37H`). Store at `40H`. 3. Reload byte. Extract lower nibble: `ANL A, #0FH`. Convert to ASCII. Store at `41H`. **Code (2 marks):** ```assembly ORG 0000H MOV A, 30H ; Load byte MOV R2, A ; Save original ANL A, #0F0H ; Keep upper nibble SWAP A ; Move to lower position ACALL HEX2ASCII ; Convert to ASCII MOV 40H, A ; Store as first char MOV A, R2 ; Restore original ANL A, #0FH ; Keep lower nibble ACALL HEX2ASCII MOV 41H, A ; Store as second char SJMP $ HEX2ASCII: MOV R3, A CLR C SUBB A, #0AH JC IS_NUM MOV A, R3 ADD A, #37H ; For A-F RET IS_NUM: MOV A, R3 ADD A, #30H ; For 0-9 RET END ``` --- #### **Question B — ASCII to Decimal and Decimal to ASCII (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]` **(a)** Design an 8051 assembly language program that accepts an **ASCII numeric digit** entered via a keypad, converts it to its **decimal value**, increments it by 1, and stores it back as an **ASCII character** in register `R2`. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Logic (3 marks):** - ASCII input is in the range `30H`–`39H`. - Step 1: Subtract `30H` → get pure decimal `0–9`. - Step 2: Add `1` to increment. - Step 3: Add `30H` → convert back to ASCII. **Code (4 marks):** ```assembly MOV A, #35H ; Suppose input is '5' = 35H MOV R2, A ; Save original ASCII CLR C SUBB A, #30H ; A = 35H - 30H = 05H (decimal 5) ADD A, #01H ; A = 06H (decimal 6) ; Note: This is pure decimal now, 0-9 only ADD A, #30H ; A = 36H = ASCII '6' MOV R2, A ; R2 = 36H = '6' END ``` **(b)** Explain with a flowchart how a **two-digit decimal number** (e.g., `47`) entered as **two ASCII characters** `'4'` and `'7'` can be converted into its **hexadecimal equivalent (`2FH`)**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Flowchart Explanation (4 marks):** ``` START ↓ Load Tens ASCII character ('4' = 34H) ↓ Subtract 30H → 04H (decimal 4) ↓ Multiply by 10 → 28H (decimal 40) ↓ Store in result register (B = 28H) ↓ Load Units ASCII character ('7' = 37H) ↓ Subtract 30H → 07H (decimal 7) ↓ Add to result → 2FH (decimal 47) ↓ Result is now in HEX form ↓ END ``` **8051 Code (3 marks):** ```assembly MOV A, #34H ; ASCII '4' CLR C SUBB A, #30H ; A = 04H MOV B, #0AH MUL AB ; B:A = 28H:00H → decimal 40 MOV R3, B ; R3 = 28H MOV A, #37H ; ASCII '7' CLR C SUBB A, #30H ; A = 07H ADD A, R3 ; A = 07H + 28H = 2FH ; A now holds 2FH = hex equivalent of 47 decimal END ``` ### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!NOTE] > **Important Things to Remember (Board Exam Quick-Reference):** - ✅ **ASCII '0' to '9' occupy `30H` to `39H`.** Add or subtract `30H` to convert between ASCII and decimal for digits. - ✅ **ASCII 'A' to 'F' occupy `41H` to `46H`.** The offset for hex letters is **`37H`**, NOT `30H`. (`41H − 0AH = 37H`). - ✅ **For lowercase hex**, use offset `57H` (`61H − 0AH = 57H`). - ✅ Always **mask with `0F0H` and `0FH`** before swapping nibbles to avoid contamination from stray bits. - ✅ The 8051 has **no `SUB` instruction** — always use `SUBB` and remember to **`CLR C`** beforehand. - ✅ The `SWAP A` instruction **exchanges upper and lower nibbles** of the accumulator in a single cycle — extremely useful for nibble extraction. - ✅ The `DIV AB` instruction divides A by B: **quotient in A, remainder in B**. Use this for decimal → hex (divide by 16) and decimal → BCD (divide by 10). - ✅ The `MUL AB` instruction multiplies A and B: **lower byte in A, upper byte in B**. - ✅ LCD displays in 8051 systems accept **ASCII codes directly** — so the final output of any conversion should ideally end up in ASCII form. - ✅ Memory layout convention in KTU labs: **input buffer at `30H–3FH`**, **output buffer at `40H–4FH`**, **temporary variables at `50H–5FH`**.

topics.title: Time delay generation and relay interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRL508 - MICROCONTROLLER LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Time delay generation and relay interface # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Time Delay Generation in 8051 Microcontroller **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** Time delay generation is the process of producing a precisely calculated waiting period between two execution events in a microcontroller program by exploiting either the CPU instruction execution time (software delay loop) or the on-chip programmable hardware timers (Timer 0 / Timer 1 in Mode 0, 1, or 2). The delay is mathematically derived using the system crystal oscillator frequency $f_{osc}$ and the *machine cycle* relationship. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the 8051 architecture, **1 Machine Cycle = 12 Oscillator Cycles**. This single fact is the foundation of every delay calculation asked in the University ESE. ### Intuitive Analogy — "The Stopwatch That Counts Clock Ticks" Imagine you are a watchmaker (the CPU) and your only tool is a mechanical pendulum that swings exactly once per second (the oscillator). To create a 1-minute delay, you simply count **60 swings**. The 8051 works the same way: - The **crystal oscillator** (say **11.0592 MHz**) is the pendulum. - The **machine cycle** (≈ **1.085 µs** at 11.0592 MHz) is each "swing that counts." - The **timer registers (TH0, TL0)** are the counter that tracks the swings. - The **overflow flag (TF0/TF1)** is the alarm bell that rings when the count is complete. ## 1.2 Relay Interface **Formal Definition:** A relay is an *electromechanically operated Single-Pole Double-Throw (SPDT)* switch whose contacts are toggled by an electromagnet. A microcontroller cannot directly energize a relay coil (typically requiring **~70 mA at 12 V or 24 V**) because its I/O pins source/sink only ~**10 mA at 5 V**. Therefore, a **driver stage** (ULN2003 Darlington array or a BC547 transistor) is interposed between the microcontroller port pin and the relay coil. An **optocoupler (MCT2E / PC817)** is also recommended for *galvanic isolation* to protect the microcontroller from back-EMF and high-voltage transients. > [!NOTE] > **Core Concept:** The microcontroller never touches the high-current load. It only sends a tiny **5 V, ~1 mA control signal** to the driver, which switches the heavy load. ### Intuitive Analogy — "The Bouncer at the Door" Think of the relay as a heavy door that requires a strong push to open. The microcontroller is a small child who *cannot* push the door directly. The child (MCU) signals a bouncer (transistor/ULN2003), and the bouncer — being strong — swings the door open. The optocoupler is like a *glass wall* between the child and the bouncer: even if the bouncer gets hurt, the child remains safe. ## 1.3 Standard Hardware Metrics You Must Memorize | Parameter | Value | KTU Relevance | |-----------|-------|---------------| | **Oscillator frequency ($f_{osc}$)** | **11.0592 MHz** (most common) | Used in UART baud rate generation | | **Machine cycle period ($T_{MC}$)** | **$\frac{12}{f_{osc}}$ = 1.085 µs** | Foundation of every delay formula | | **Timer resolution** | **1 machine cycle = 1.085 µs** | Minimum delay granularity | | **8-bit timer overflow count** | **256 (= $2^8$)** | Determines maximum delay in Mode 0 / 1 | | **16-bit timer overflow count** | **65536 (= $2^{16}$)** | Determines maximum delay in Mode 1 | | **Auto-reload (Mode 2) range** | **0 to 255** | 8-bit auto-reload timer | | **Relay coil current (typical 5V relay)** | **~70 mA** | Exceeds MCU pin capability | | **ULN2003 output current (per channel)** | **500 mA** | Standard relay driver IC | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Software Delay vs Hardware Timer Delay ### A. Software Delay (Nested Loop Method) - Implemented purely using `for` or `while` loop instructions. - Delay depends on the *number of instructions executed* × *instruction time*. - **Disadvantage:** CPU is *blocked* — cannot perform any other task during the delay. Inefficient for multi-tasking. ### B. Hardware Timer Delay - Uses on-chip **Timer 0** or **Timer 1** registers (`TH0`, `TL0`). - Timer runs *independently* of the CPU. CPU can do other jobs or enter **idle mode**. - Timer is started, CPU polls the `TF0` overflow flag, executes the required action, and resets the timer. ## 2.2 The 8051 Timer Operational Logic (Step-by-Step) 1. **Load `TMOD`** — Select **Mode** (Mode 0 = 13-bit, Mode 1 = 16-bit, Mode 2 = 8-bit auto-reload) and operation (Timer or Counter). 2. **Load initial count** into `TH0` and `TL0` (the "starting number" of the stopwatch). 3. **Start the timer** by setting the `TR0` bit in the `TCON` register. 4. **Timer increments** on every machine cycle until it overflows from `FFFFh → 0000h` (Mode 1) or `FFh → 00h` (Mode 0/Mode 2). 5. **Hardware sets the `TF0` (Timer 0 Overflow Flag)** automatically. 6. **CPU detects `TF0 = 1`**, executes the delayed action, and clears `TF0`. 7. **Stop the timer** by clearing `TR0`. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Overflow" instead of "Count Up"?** Since the timer counts upward from the initial value to the maximum (FFFFh), the *number of timer ticks* is calculated as **(Maximum value) − (Initial value) + 1**. This is a classic KTU trap — students often forget the **+1**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes | |---|----------|---------|------|-------| | 1 | Machine cycle period | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds | For 11.0592 MHz → **1.085 µs** | | 2 | Timer tick duration | $T_{tick} = 1 \times T_{MC}$ | seconds | One increment = 1.085 µs | | 3 | Required timer ticks | $N_{ticks} = \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{tick}}$ | dimensionless | Total counts needed | | 4 | Timer reload value (Mode 1, 16-bit) | $Reload = 65536 - N_{ticks}$ | hex | Load into `TH0` (high byte) and `TL0` (low byte) | | 5 | Timer reload value (Mode 2, 8-bit) | $Reload = 256 - N_{ticks}$ | hex | Load into `TH0`; `TL0` is auto-managed | | 6 | Maximum delay (Mode 1, no prescaler) | $T_{max} = 65536 \times T_{MC}$ | seconds | For 11.0592 MHz → **71.11 ms** | | 7 | Maximum delay (Mode 2) | $T_{max} = 256 \times T_{MC}$ | seconds | For 11.0592 MHz → **277.76 µs** | | 8 | Hex byte conversion | High byte of $Reload \Rightarrow TH0$ ; Low byte $\Rightarrow TL0$ | — | Use manual division by 256 | | 9 | ULN2003 input voltage | $V_{IN(min)} \approx 1.5\,V$ | volts | Logic HIGH drives relay ON | | 10 | Relay back-EMF | $V_{flyback} = -L \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | volts | Suppressed by flyback diode (1N4007) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Anti-pipe safeguard:** Absolute value bars in the table above are intentionally written as `\vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial Automation:** Time delays control the sequence of conveyor belts, motor starters, and traffic lights. - **Domestic Appliances:** Washing machines use timed relays to switch agitation direction every 30 seconds. - **Power Electronics:** Solid-State Relays (SSRs) and electromechanical relays switch AC loads in UPS systems, with software-controlled debounce delays. - **Automotive Electronics:** ECU-controlled relays engage fuel pumps, headlights, and starter motors with millisecond precision. - **IoT & Smart Home:** ESP32/8051-driven relays switch mains appliances via Wi-Fi with anti-flutter delays to prevent relay contact chattering. # SECTION_3 START — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Compute Timer Reload Value for a Given Delay (Mode 1, 16-bit) **Given:** - Crystal frequency $f_{osc} = 11.0592\,\text{MHz}$ - Required delay $T_{delay} = 50\,\text{ms}$ **Find:** Hex value to load into `TH0` and `TL0`. **Step 1 — Machine Cycle Period:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{MC} &= \frac{12}{f_{osc}} \\ &= \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \\ &= 1.085\,\mu s \end{aligned} $$ *Conversion logic:* Divide 12 by the oscillator frequency in Hz. This gives the period of one machine cycle in seconds. **Step 2 — Total Number of Timer Ticks Required:** $$ \begin{aligned} N_{ticks} &= \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{tick}} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}} \\ &= \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \\ &= 46{,}082.95 \approx 46{,}083 \text{ counts} \end{aligned} $$ *Conversion logic:* Since each timer increment consumes exactly one machine cycle (1.085 µs), divide the required total delay by the time per tick to obtain the number of increments. **Step 3 — Compute 16-bit Reload Value (Mode 1):** $$ \begin{aligned} Reload_{16} &= 65{,}536 - N_{ticks} \\ &= 65{,}536 - 46{,}083 \\ &= 19{,}453 \end{aligned} $$ *Conversion logic:* A 16-bit timer overflows at 65,536. To count *exactly* $N_{ticks}$ times, we must preload the counter with this value. **Step 4 — Convert Reload Value to Hex:** $$ \begin{aligned} 19{,}453 \div 256 &= 76 \text{ remainder } -3 \quad \text{→ recompute:} \\ 19{,}453 \div 256 &= 75 \text{ remainder } 253 \\ \text{High byte} &= 75_{10} = 4B_{H} \\ \text{Low byte} &= 253_{10} = FD_{H} \end{aligned} $$ *Conversion logic:* Divide the decimal reload value by 256. Quotient = TH0, Remainder = TL0. Convert both to hex. **Final Timer Initialization Code:** ```c TMOD = 0x01; // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit) TH0 = 0x4B; // High byte of reload TL0 = 0xFD; // Low byte of reload TR0 = 1; // Start Timer 0 while (TF0 == 0); // Wait for overflow flag TR0 = 0; // Stop timer TF0 = 0; // Clear overflow flag ``` ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Achievable Delay in Mode 1 (No Prescaler) **Given:** $f_{osc} = 12\,\text{MHz}$ (standard KTU lab crystal). **Step 1 — Machine Cycle Period:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{MC} &= \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1\,\mu s \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Maximum Tick Count (Reload = 0):** $$ \begin{aligned} N_{ticks(max)} &= 65{,}536 - 0 = 65{,}536 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Maximum Delay:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{delay(max)} &= 65{,}536 \times 1\,\mu s = 65.536\,\text{ms} \end{aligned} $$ *Conversion logic:* When reload value = 0, the timer counts through all 65,536 states (1 to 65,536) before overflowing, giving the longest possible delay in Mode 1. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Generating a 1-Second Delay Using Nested Loops with Timer A single timer overflow gives at most ~71 ms. To get 1 second, we *repeat* the 50 ms overflow approximately 20 times. **Step 1 — Number of Repetitions:** $$ \begin{aligned} N_{reps} &= \frac{1000\,\text{ms}}{50\,\text{ms}} = 20 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Implementation Snippet:** ```c void delay_1sec(void) { unsigned char i; for (i = 0; i < 20; i++) { TMOD = 0x01; // Timer 0, Mode 1 TH0 = 0x4B; TL0 = 0xFD; // ~50 ms delay (assumes 11.0592 MHz) TR0 = 1; while (TF0 == 0); TR0 = 0; TF0 = 0; } } ``` *Conversion logic:* Outer software loop multiplies the inner hardware timer's 50 ms delay by 20 to reach exactly 1 second. This is the **standard KTU pattern** for long delays. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Relay Switching Time Analysis A relay has two important timing parameters: **operate time ($t_{op}$)** and **release time ($t_{rel}$)**. $$ \begin{aligned} t_{op} &\approx 5\text{ to }15\,\text{ms} \\ t_{rel} &\approx 3\text{ to }10\,\text{ms} \\ t_{bounce} &\leq 2\,\text{ms (mechanical contact)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical Rule:** After energizing or de-energizing a relay, the software **must wait at least 20 ms** before sensing the contact state, to account for operate time + bounce. This is why relay interface programs always include a delay loop between switching and sensing. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Delay Methods Comparison | Method | Range | Accuracy | CPU Blocking | Use Case | |--------|-------|----------|--------------|----------| | **Software loop** (empty `for`) | µs to seconds | ±5–10% (compiler dependent) | **Yes — 100%** | Simple, non-critical delays | | **Timer Mode 0 (13-bit)** | Up to ~8.19 ms @ 12 MHz | ±1 machine cycle | **No** | Legacy, rarely used | | **Timer Mode 1 (16-bit)** | Up to ~71.11 ms @ 11.0592 MHz | ±1 machine cycle | **No** | Most common KTU lab choice | | **Timer Mode 2 (auto-reload)** | Up to ~277.76 µs @ 11.0592 MHz | ±1 machine cycle | **No** | UART baud rate generator | | **Timer + Software Counter** | Any long delay | ±1 machine cycle × loop count | **Mostly idle** | 1-second buzzers, motor sequencing | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: 8051 ↔ ULN2003 ↔ Relay ↔ Load ```mermaid flowchart LR MCU["8051 Microcontroller
P1.0 Control Pin
5V TTL Logic"] OPTO["Optocoupler MCT2E
Galvanic Isolation
1.5V to 5V Input"] DRV["ULN2003 Darlington
Driver Array
500mA per channel"] RELAY["Electromechanical
Relay SPDT
12V coil, 70mA"] DIODE["1N4007 Flyback
Diode across coil"] LOAD["AC or DC Load
Lamp / Motor / Fan"] VCC["12V DC Supply
for Relay Coil"] MCU -- "Logic HIGH 5V" --> OPTO OPTO -- "Isolated LOW drive" --> DRV DRV -- "Sinks ~70mA" --> RELAY RELAY -- "Common to NO contact closes" --> LOAD VCC -. "Coil supply" .-> RELAY DIODE -. "Suppresses back-EMF" .-> RELAY ``` *Reading the diagram:* When the 8051 sets P1.0 HIGH, the optocoupler LED turns ON, the phototransistor conducts, pulling the ULN2003 input LOW. The ULN2003 output then sinks the relay coil current to ground, energizing the coil. The COMMON-to-NO contact closes, applying the high-voltage supply to the load. ## 4.2 Timer Operation State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle: System Reset Idle --> LoadTimer: Configure TMOD, TH0, TL0 LoadTimer --> Running: Set TR0 = 1 Running --> Running: Increment on
each machine cycle Running --> Overflow: Counter reaches FFFFh Overflow --> FlagSet: Hardware sets TF0 = 1 FlagSet --> Action: CPU executes
delayed task Action --> StopTimer: Clear TR0 and TF0 StopTimer --> Idle: Ready for next delay Idle --> [*] ``` ## 4.3 Complete Relay Interface Circuit (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph POWER["Power Supply Section"] X1["230V AC Mains"] X2["Step-Down Transformer
230V to 12V-0-12V"] X3["Bridge Rectifier
1N4007 x 4"] X4["7805 Regulator
5V to MCU"] X5["LM7812 Regulator
12V to Relay Coil"] end subgraph CONTROL["Control Section"] M1["8051 Microcontroller
P1.0 = Relay Control"] M2["Crystal 11.0592 MHz
+ 33pF Caps"] M3["EA# tied HIGH
Port 0 Pull-ups 10k"] end subgraph DRIVER["Driver Section"] D1["Optocoupler PC817
Input: 5V via 330R"] D2["ULN2003 Channel
Output to Relay"] D3["Flyback Diode
1N4007 across coil"] end subgraph ACTUATOR["Actuator and Load"] R1["12V SPDT Relay
Coil 700 ohms"] L1["AC Load 230V
Lamp or Motor"] end X1 --> X2 --> X3 --> X4 X3 --> X5 X4 --> M1 M2 --> M1 M1 -- "P1.0 HIGH" --> D1 D1 --> D2 D2 --> R1 D3 --> R1 X5 --> R1 R1 -- "NO contact closes" --> L1 ``` ## 4.4 Software Flowchart: Relay Toggle with 1-Second Delay ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Start]) B[Initialize Port 1 as output] C[Set P1.0 = 1
Relay ON] D[Call delay 1 second] E[Set P1.0 = 0
Relay OFF] F[Call delay 1 second] G{Loop
Forever?} H([End]) A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G G -- "Yes" --> C G -- "No" --> H ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define machine cycle. Calculate the machine cycle period for an 8051 system with a crystal frequency of 11.0592 MHz.** **Model Answer:** A *machine cycle* is the basic unit of time for instruction execution in the 8051. It is the time taken to complete one memory access and is equal to **12 oscillator periods**. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{MC} &= \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \\ &= 1.085 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} \\ &= 1.085\,\mu s \end{aligned} $$ **Mark Distribution (as per KTU valuation key):** - Definition: 1 mark - Formula: 1 mark - Final numerical answer: 1 mark --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Why is a driver IC like ULN2003 required between an 8051 port pin and a relay coil? Mention the role of the flyback diode.** **Model Answer:** The 8051 port pin can source/sink only about **10 mA** at 5 V, whereas a typical relay coil requires **~70 mA at 12 V**. Directly connecting the coil to the MCU pin will (i) fail to energize the relay, and (ii) damage the port pin. Hence, a **ULN2003 Darlington driver array** is used to provide the necessary current amplification (up to **500 mA per channel**). When the relay coil is de-energized, the collapsing magnetic field induces a high-voltage *back-EMF* ($V = -L\,di/dt$) that can destroy the driver transistor.
